chunks.text: on 17 March 2022. The Government has said the Bill delivers its “manifesto commitment to make the UK the safest place in the world to be online while defending free expression”. Warning: This briefing discusses child sexual abuse and sexual exploitation, suicide and self-harm, which some readers may find distressing. What does the Online Safety Bill aim to do? The Bill has five policy objectives: • to increase user safety online. • to preserve and enhance freedom of speech online. • to improve law enforcement’s ability to tackle illegal content online. • to improve users’ ability to keep themselves safe online. • to improve society’s understanding of the harm landscape. Policy background to the Bill, as it was originally introduced, is set out in the Library briefing, Analysis of the Online Safety Bill (PDF)(8 April 2022). Progress of the Bill in the House of Commons The Bill [Bill 285 2021-22] had its second reading on 19 April 2022. A carry-over motion, allowing it to be carried into the next parliamentary session, was approved on the same date. The Bill was considered by a Public Bill Committee over seventeen sittings between 24 May and 28 June 2022. Oral evidence was taken from expert witnesses during the first four sittings. Line by line examination of the Bill took place over thirteen sittings in June 2022. The Government added a new schedule 2 to the Bill to enable Ofcom to recover its initial costs by charging fees to service providers. Clause 129 was also amended by the Government so that Ofcom would have 6 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

The Bill has five policy objectives: • to increase user safety online. • to preserve and enhance freedom of speech online. • to improve law enforcement’s ability to tackle illegal content online. • to improve users’ ability to keep themselves safe online. • to improve society’s understanding of the harm landscape. Policy background to the Bill, as it was originally introduced, is set out in the Library briefing, Analysis of the Online Safety Bill (PDF)(8 April 2022). Progress of the Bill in the House of Commons The Bill [Bill 285 2021-22] had its second reading on 19 April 2022. A carry-over motion, allowing it to be carried into the next parliamentary session, was approved on the same date. The Bill was considered by a Public Bill Committee over seventeen sittings between 24 May and 28 June 2022. Oral evidence was taken from expert witnesses during the first four sittings. Line by line examination of the Bill took place over thirteen sittings in June 2022. The Government added a new schedule 2 to the Bill to enable Ofcom to recover its initial costs by charging fees to service providers. Clause 129 was also amended by the Government so that Ofcom would have 6 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: a new schedule 2 to the Bill to enable Ofcom to recover its initial costs by charging fees to service providers. Clause 129 was also amended by the Government so that Ofcom would have 6 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill to consult the Information Commissioner’s Office before publishing guidance on using its enforcement powers. No Opposition amendments were agreed. The first day of report stage took place on 12 July 2022. Government amendments were added to the Bill relating to journalistic content, adult safety duties, and illegal content duties. When Michelle Donelan became Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) on 6 September 2022, she reviewed the Bill’s adult safety duties. According to the DCMS, this was to ensure the right balance was struck “between protecting users from harmful content online and protecting users’ rights to freedom of expression”. During this time, some MPs raised concerns about when the Bill’s remaining Commons stages would take place and whether it would have time to pass in the current parliamentary session. The second day of report stage took place on 5 December 2022. New Government clauses and amendments were agreed. These included a new clause to add an offence of “epilepsy trolling” to the Bill. The Commons also agreed several Government amendments to remove the proposed new harmful communications offence and the proposed repeal of existing harmful communication offences. On 28 November 2022, the Government announced plans to amend the Bill, including the removal of the “legal but harmful” provisions for adults to protect freedom of expression. To enable debate on the amendments and new clauses, the Bill was recommitted to a Public Bill Committee. There were two sittings on 13 December 2022 and one sitting on 15 December 2022. New Government clauses and amendments were made. Among other things, these removed the adult safety duties and introduced new user empowerment tools for adults, so people would be able to control what content they might see online. They would also require the largest companies to remove or restrict access to legal content only where this was consistent with their terms of service. The Bill had its third day of report stage and its third reading on 17 January 2023.

The Bill had its third day of report stage and its third reading on 17 January 2023. Progress of the Bill in the House of Lords The Bill was introduced in the House of Lords on 18 January 2023 (HL Bill 87(Rev) (PDF). Lords second reading took place on 1 February 2023. The Bill had sixteen sittings in Committee between 25 April and 22 June 2023 where Government amendments were agreed. These included a new clause that would make it an offence to encourage or assist another person to seriously self-harm. No Opposition amendments were agreed. 7 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill The Bill was considered on report on 6, 10, 12, 17 and 19 July 2023. Government amendments were made to the Bill. These would, among other things: • require the use of age verification or age estimation to prevent children accessing pornographic content. • put the categories of ‘primary priority’ and ‘priority’ content that is harmful to children on the face of the Bill. • enable Ofcom to require information relating to a child’s social media use if requested to do so by a coroner. • strengthen the user empowerment features by requiring Category 1 providers to ask their registered adult users whether they would like the content tools to be applied. • require Ofcom to produce guidance summarising measures that could be taken to reduce the risk of harm to women and girls. •

Government amendments were made to the Bill. These would, among other things: • require the use of age verification or age estimation to prevent children accessing pornographic content. • put the categories of ‘primary priority’ and ‘priority’ content that is harmful to children on the face of the Bill. • enable Ofcom to require information relating to a child’s social media use if requested to do so by a coroner. • strengthen the user empowerment features by requiring Category 1 providers to ask their registered adult users whether they would like the content tools to be applied. • require Ofcom to produce guidance summarising measures that could be taken to reduce the risk of harm to women and girls. •

chunks.text: • strengthen the user empowerment features by requiring Category 1 providers to ask their registered adult users whether they would like the content tools to be applied. • require Ofcom to produce guidance summarising measures that could be taken to reduce the risk of harm to women and girls. • create a new offence that would hold senior managers liable for a provider’s failure to comply with a child sexual exploitation and abuse requirement imposed by a confirmation decision. • update Ofcom’s statutory media literacy duty to introduce new objectives relating to regulated services. • introduce new offences of sharing or threatening to share intimate photographs or films. The Government was defeated on three issues at report stage: 8 • adding animal welfare issues to schedule 7 (Priority offences). This became clause 64 of the Bill as amended on Report (HL Bill 164 (PDF)). Baroness Merron (Shadow Spokesperson for Culture, Media and Sport) said the new clause was needed because of the “worryingly high volume of animal cruelty and animal torture content” available online. • an amendment to clause 11 (Children’s risk assessment duties) of HL Bill 164. The amendment would mean that in carrying out risk assessments, user-to-user services would have to “consider the potential for the design and operation of services to create harm separately and additionally to harm relating to the dissemination of or encountering harmful content”. Baroness Kidron (Crossbench) said her amendment was necessary as the Government would not accept that harm to children could arise from the functionality and design of services and not just from content. • an amendment to schedule 11 (Categories of regulated user-to-user services and regulated search services: regulations). This would mean that the test for determining which services would fall within Category Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023

This became clause 64 of the Bill as amended on Report (HL Bill 164 (PDF)). Baroness Merron (Shadow Spokesperson for Culture, Media and Sport) said the new clause was needed because of the “worryingly high volume of animal cruelty and animal torture content” available online. • an amendment to clause 11 (Children’s risk assessment duties) of HL Bill 164. The amendment would mean that in carrying out risk assessments, user-to-user services would have to “consider the potential for the design and operation of services to create harm separately and additionally to harm relating to the dissemination of or encountering harmful content”. Baroness Kidron (Crossbench) said her amendment was necessary as the Government would not accept that harm to children could arise from the functionality and design of services and not just from content. • an amendment to schedule 11 (Categories of regulated user-to-user services and regulated search services: regulations). This would mean that the test for determining which services would fall within Category Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023

chunks.text: arise from the functionality and design of services and not just from content. • an amendment to schedule 11 (Categories of regulated user-to-user services and regulated search services: regulations). This would mean that the test for determining which services would fall within Category Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 1 would move from a test of size “and” functionality to a test of size “or” functionality. Baroness Morgan of Cotes (Labour) said her amendment was needed because functionality and not just size could make platforms harmful. The Bill had its third reading on 6 September 2023. Ping pong The House of Commons considered Lords amendments to the Bill on 12 September 2023. The Government agreed to the amendment to schedule 11. It also agreed to add animal welfare offences to schedule 7. On amending clause 11, Paul Scully, Minister for Tech and the Digital Economy, said the Government agreed with the objective, but was concerned that Baroness Kidron’s amendment might not work within the Bill’s framework. The Government had therefore worked closely with Baroness Kidron and others to draft its own amendments. These would make it explicit that service providers would be required to assess the impact that design, functionalities, and other features had on the risk of harm to children. The House of Lords agreed with the amendments on 19 September 2023. Royal Assent The Bill received Royal Assent on 26 October 2023. The Online Safety Act 2023 (PDF) is available online. A Government press release summarises the main protections the Act will provide. Ofcom, the regulator, has published details on the implementation of the Act: 9 • Online safety rules: what you need to know. • Ofcom's approach to implementing the Online Safety Act.

Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 1 Commons second reading The Bill had its second reading in the House of Commons on 19 April 2022. Lucy Powell, then Shadow Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport, said the Bill was “an important step towards strong, independent regulation”. 1 However, she claimed the Bill’s attempt to balance free speech and regulation would “please no one”: …Some believe the Bill will stifle free speech, with platforms over-zealously taking down legitimate political and other views. In response, the Government have put in what they consider to be protections for freedom of speech and have committed to setting out an exhaustive list of “legal but harmful” content, thus relying almost entirely on a “take down content” approach, which many will still see as Government overreach. On the other hand, those who want harmful outcomes addressed through stronger regulation are left arguing over a yet-to-be-published list of Government-determined harmful content. This content-driven approach moves us in the wrong direction away from the “duty of care” principles the Bill is supposed to enshrine… 2 Lucy Powell said the solution was a systems-based approach that would make the Bill “much stronger”. Instead of “going after” individual content, such an approach would take on the business models, systems and policies that drive the impact of harmful content. 3 She also raised concerns in the following areas: 1 2 3 4 5 6 10 •

chunks.text: Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill • the metaverse and new technologies could mean the Bill quickly becoming out of date. 7 In contrast, an approach focused on outcomes rather than content would create a more dynamic system of regulation. 8 Julian Knight, then Chair of the Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee, said the Bill was “important and improved”. 9 However, he was concerned about the risks of cross-platform grooming and “bread- crumbing” to trap children into a sequence of abuse. 10 He also said some of the Bill’s provisions should apply to smaller platforms, not just the largest Category 1 services. 11 Julian Knight was also concerned about the Bill’s definition of “news publishers” and said Ofcom shouldn’t become a regulator or a licensing body for the press. 12 Damian Collins, Chair of the Joint Committee on the draft Bill, said the Bill did cover the systems, including algorithms, of social media companies. 13 He also said the metaverse and virtual reality platforms were within scope. Damian Collins defended the Bill’s approach to “legal but harmful” content and argued that Ofcom’s threshold for intervention should be based on case law. 14 Jamie Stone, the Liberal Democrat Spokesperson for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport, supported the thrust of the Bill. However, he was “slightly” concerned about the vagueness of some of the Bill’s definitions (eg harmful content) and that secondary legislation, that many Members of Parliament did not take a “huge amount” of interest in, would be used to set out further detail. 15 John Nicolson, the Scottish National Party spokesperson for Digital, Culture Media and Sport, said he wanted the Bill to succeed. 16 However, he raised concerns about: • 7 8 9 10 11 12

HC Deb 19 April 2022 cc128-9 HC Deb 19 April 2022 c107 HC Deb 19 April 2022 c107 HC Deb 19 April 2022 c108 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 2 Commons Public Bill Committee The Bill was considered by a Public Bill Committee over seventeen sittings between 24 May and 28 June 2022. Oral evidence was taken from expert witnesses during the first four sittings. Line by line examination of the Bill took place over thirteen sittings in June 2022. This section of the Briefing looks at the clauses on which the Committee divided or where Government amendments were made. Further detail on the clauses discussed below can be found in the Library Briefing, Analysis of the Online Safety Bill (PDF)(8 April 2022) and the Explanatory Notes to the Bill (PDF). 2.1 Parts 3 and 4 of the Bill: duties of regulated services Part 3 of the Bill sets out the duties that would apply to “regulated services” (ie user-to-user services and search services with “links” to the UK). All regulated services would have to tackle illegal content. They would also have to assess whether their services were likely to be accessed by children and, if so, protect children from harmful content. The largest and riskiest (Category 1) service providers would have duties in relation to legal but harmful content to adults. Category 1 and the largest (Category 2A) search services would have a fraudulent advertising duty.

All regulated services would have to tackle illegal content. They would also have to assess whether their services were likely to be accessed by children and, if so, protect children from harmful content. Category 1 and the largest (Category 2A) search services would have a fraudulent advertising duty.

Criminal liability for failing to comply with safety duties When clause 6 (providers of user-to-user services: duties of care) was debated, the Shadow Minister for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport, Alex Davies-Jones, moved amendment 69. 23 This would require regulated companies to designate a senior manager as a safety controller who would be responsible for ensuring the service met its illegality risk assessment and content safety duties. The safety manager would be criminally liable for “significant and egregious failures to protect users from harms”. 24 When speaking to the amendment, Alex Davies-Jones pointed out that the Bill would introduce criminal liability for senior managers who failed to comply 23 24 13 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 cc212-5 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c213 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

The safety manager would be criminally liable for “significant and egregious failures to protect users from harms”. 24 When speaking to the amendment, Alex Davies-Jones pointed out that the Bill would introduce criminal liability for senior managers who failed to comply 23 24 13 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 cc212-5 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c213 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Alex Davies-Jones pointed out that the Bill would introduce criminal liability for senior managers who failed to comply 23 24 13 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 cc212-5 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c213 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill with information notice provisions. However, there was no such liability for failing to fulfil their statutory safety duties and yet such failures could lead to the most seriously harmful outcomes. She said this was a “disgrace”. 25 Amendment 69 would “drive a more effective culture of online safety awareness within regulated firms by making senior management accountable for harms caused through their platforms and embedding safety within governance structures”. 26 Kirsty Blackman (Scottish National Party) supported the amendment. 27 For the Government, Chris Philp, then a DCMS Minister, referred to some of the current enforcement provisions in the Bill – eg senior management liability relating to non-compliance with information notices and offences of falsifying, encrypting or destroying information. These offences, punishable by a prison sentence of up to two years, were “critical” because Ofcom would be unable to carry out its enforcement activities without that information. 28 Ofcom would also be able to fine non-compliant companies up to £18 million or 10% of global revenue. In certain circumstances, Ofcom could apply to the courts for “business disruption measures”. Chris Philp said he had been “debating internally” whether there should be further personal criminal liability for breaches of duties other than those relating to providing information. He put forward two arguments against: • introducing criminal liability for other duties would risk social media firms, fearing criminal prosecution, becoming “over-zealous” and taking everything down. This could have “a chilling effect on content available online”. •

chunks.text: liability for breaches of duties other than those relating to providing information. He put forward two arguments against: • introducing criminal liability for other duties would risk social media firms, fearing criminal prosecution, becoming “over-zealous” and taking everything down. This could have “a chilling effect on content available online”. • it would be “pretty obvious” what an individual executive would have to do to comply with duties to provide information. However, with some of the Bill’s other duties, it would be “less obvious” which made it “harder to justify expanding criminal liability to those circumstances”. Chris Philp therefore said the Bill “struck the balance in the right place”. 29 Kirsty Blackman suggested that, rather than accepting the amendment, the Government could consider using secondary legislation to make changes, if needed in the future, to individual criminal liability for different breaches. 30 Chris Philp said creating new criminal offences through secondary legislation 25 26 27 28 29 30 14 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 cc213-4 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c214 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c213 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c216 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 cc217-8 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c218 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill might be “viewed askance” by some colleagues but he would reflect on the suggestion. 31 Amendment 69 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 6. 32

chunks.text: cc217-8 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c218 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill might be “viewed askance” by some colleagues but he would reflect on the suggestion. 31 Amendment 69 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 6. 32 Illegal content risk assessments Clause 8 of the Bill would require regulated user-to-user services to carry out an “illegal content risk assessment”. When clause 8 was debated, Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 10. This would create a duty for regulated services to publish an illegal content risk assessment and supply it to Ofcom. When speaking to the amendment, Alex Davies-Jones said that, under clause 8, illegal content risk assessments would only be made available to Ofcom. She said this lack of transparency could undermine the effectiveness of the Bill. The risk assessments should be made available for public scrutiny. 33 Amendments 14, 25 and 19 were also considered. Amendment 14 would seek to ensure that regulated companies’ boards or senior staff had appropriate oversight of risk assessments related to adults. Alex-Davies Jones said this would “hardwire the safety duties and create a culture of compliance in the regulated firms”. 34 Amendment 25 would require the risk assessment to consider the risk of the production of illegal content, as well as the risk of its presence and dissemination. Alex Davies-Jones said this was necessary because clause 8 did not “go far enough to tackle newly produced content or livestreamed content”. She referred to the livestreaming of sexual exploitation of children (“a severely under-reported crime”) and the livestreaming of terror attacks. 35 Amendment 19 would incorporate into clause 8 a requirement for regulated services to consider cross-platform risk. Alex Davies-Jones said charities such as the NSPCC and 5Rights had raised concerns about a gap in the Bill in relation to cases where children were groomed on one platform, where no abuse took place, but were then directed to another platform, where they were harmed.36 She said companies needed to understand, among other things, how their platforms were “situated on abuse pathways whereby the grooming and other online sexual abuse risks start on their site before migrating to other services, or whether they inherit risks from other sites”. 37 In response to amendment 10, Chris Philp said requiring 25,000 in-scope services to deliver a copy of their risk assessment, even if they were very low

He also pointed out that Ofcom would be able to ask for any risk assessment if it had concerns about a platform. If this duty was not met, senior manager liability (with a possible two years in prison) would apply.38 Chris Philp also said the publication of a full risk assessment would publicly expose the vulnerabilities of a company’s systems, or the potential opportunities to exploit those systems for criminal purposes. This would not be in the public interest and was a reason for “not requiring complete disclosure of everything”.39 The Minister pointed out that the transparency reporting requirement for larger companies (clause 64) would give Ofcom wide powers to specify what would have to be included in the report. 40 In response to amendment 14, Chris Philp said the risk assessment duties already required companies to consider how their governance structures could affect the risk of harm to users arising from illegal content. Ofcom would issue guidance to companies on how to comply with the duties and this was “very likely to include measures relating to senior-level engagement”. 41 In response to amendment 19 on cross-platform risk, Chris Philp said clause 8 already covered this because platforms would have to risk-assess illegal content that might be encountered “by means of the service”. This phrase meant not just on the service itself but, potentially, via that service. 42 In response to amendment 25, Chris Philp said clause 8(5)(d) of the Bill would already require services to risk-assess functionalities of their service being used to facilitate the presence of illegal content. 43 Amendment 10 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 44 Amendment 14 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 45 Amendment 25 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 46 Amendment 19 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 47 Alex Davies-Jones also moved amendment 17. Alex Davies-Jones moved several amendments to this clause. Amendment 18 sought to bring measures to minimise content that may facilitate or aid the discovery of priority illegal content within the scope of the duty to maintain proportionate systems and processes. 51 Alex Davies-Jones said the amendment was needed because the Bill “did not capture the range of ways in which child abusers use social networks to organise abuse, including to form offender networks”: …They post digital breadcrumbs that signpost to illegal content on third-party messaging apps and the dark web, and they share child abuse videos that are carefully edited to fall within content moderation guidelines. This range of techniques, known as child abuse breadcrumbing, is a significant enabler of online child abuse. 52
chunks.display_text: Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill facilitate or aid the discovery or dissemination of child sexual exploitation and abuse (CSEA) content. 48 Chris Philp said clause 8 already did this. 49 Amendment 17 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 50 Safety duties about illegal content Clause 9 of the Bill sets out the duties that would apply to regulated user-touser services in relation to illegal content. Alex Davies-Jones moved several amendments to this clause. Amendment 18 sought to bring measures to minimise content that may facilitate or aid the discovery of priority illegal content within the scope of the duty to maintain proportionate systems and processes. 51 Alex Davies-Jones said the amendment was needed because the Bill “did not capture the range of ways in which child abusers use social networks to organise abuse, including to form offender networks”: …They post digital breadcrumbs that signpost to illegal content on third-party messaging apps and the dark web, and they share child abuse videos that are carefully edited to fall within content moderation guidelines. This range of techniques, known as child abuse breadcrumbing, is a significant enabler of online child abuse. 52

chunks.text: post digital breadcrumbs that signpost to illegal content on third-party messaging apps and the dark web, and they share child abuse videos that are carefully edited to fall within content moderation guidelines. This range of techniques, known as child abuse breadcrumbing, is a significant enabler of online child abuse. 52 According to Alex Davies-Jones, the amendment would give Ofcom powers to “tackle breadcrumbing and ensure a proactive upstream response”. It would also ensure that companies would have to tackle child abuse at the earliest possible stage. 53 For the Government, Chris Philp said companies in scope would be obliged to take comprehensive measures to tackle CSEA content, including where a service directed users on the first service to the second service. 54 Amendment 18 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 55 Amendment 21 would require platforms to collaborate in cases where there was potential cross-platform risk in relation to priority illegal content and CSEA content. 56 When speaking to the amendment, Alex Davies-Jones said child abuse was “rarely siloed on a single platform or app” and that abusers exploited the design features of social networks to contact children before they moved communication to other platforms, including livestreaming sites 48 49 50 51 52 53 54 55 56 17 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c243 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c244 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c245 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c234 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c239 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c239 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c238 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c246 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c234 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

Barbara Keeley (Labour) moved amendment 15 to ensure that regulated companies’ boards or senior staff would have responsibility for children’s risk assessments.63 Amendment 16 to clause 25 was considered at the same time. This sought to ensure that the boards or senior staff of regulated search services would have responsibility for children’s risk assessments. 64 When speaking to amendment 15, Barbara Keeley welcomed the duty to riskassess user-to-user services likely to be accessed by children. However, this would “become an empty, tick-box exercise” unless there was a requirement for boards to review and approve the assessments. 65 A requirement to have the board or a senior manager approve the risk assessment would “hardwire the safety duties into decision making and create accountability and responsibility at the most senior level of the organisation”. This should then “trickle down the organisation and help embed a culture of compliance across it.” 66 Chris Philp said clause 10 already addressed governance arrangements: 57 58 59 60 61 62 63 64 65 66 18

This should then “trickle down the organisation and help embed a culture of compliance across it.” 66 Chris Philp said clause 10 already addressed governance arrangements: 57 58 59 60 61 62 63 64 65 66 18 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c240 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c240 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c238 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c247 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c234 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c246 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c247 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 cc247-8 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c248 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c248 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill First, the duties set out in clause 10(6)(h) in relation to children’s risk assessments already require the governance structures to be properly considered, so governance is directly addressed. Secondly, subsection (2) states that the risk assessment has to be “suitable and sufficient”, so it cannot be done in a perfunctory or slipshod way. Again, Ofcom must be satisfied that those governance arrangements are appropriate. We could invent all the governance arrangements in the world, but the outcome needs to be delivered and, in this case, to protect children. 67

Kim Leadbeater (Labour) said fining companies would not tackle the root of the problem and that cultural change and leadership at companies was needed.68 Alex Davies-Jones said if a platform was shut down, it would just set up another one, and this was already happening with some of the smaller ones. However, introducing personal liability and holding people personally responsible for the direct harm they caused would make a difference.69 Amendment 15 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 70 Amendment 16 was negatived on division by 10 votes to 7. 71 Adults’ risk assessment duties Clause 12 of the Bill would require Category 1 service providers to carry out an adults’ risk assessment. Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 12 to create a duty to publish the adults’ risk assessment and supply it to Ofcom. 72 She said it was “only fair” that information about risks to personal safety on Category 1 services – the most popular and often “the most troublesome services” – should be available to users. 73 In response, Chris Philp said other clauses of the Bill dealt with the transparency issues being raised: …there is a duty in clause 13(2) for platforms to publish in their terms of service—a public document—the findings of the most recent adult risk assessment. That duty is in clause 13—the next clause we are going to debate—in addition to the obligations I have referred to twice already in clause 64, where Ofcom compels those firms to publish their transparency reports. chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c253 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c256 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c304 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c263 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c263 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill that through clause 13(2) and clause 64, those objectives are met in the Bill as drafted. 74 Amendment 12 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 75 User empowerment duties Under clauses 14(2) and (3) of the Bill, Category 1 services would need to have features in place to allow adult users to control what priority legal but harmful content they could see. These features would: (a) reduce the likelihood of the user encountering “priority content that was harmful to adults”, or particular kinds of such content, by means of the service; or (b) alert the user to the harmful nature of priority content that was harmful to adults that the user may encounter by means of the service. Under clauses 14(6) and (7), Category 1 services would need to have features in place to: (a) prevent non-verified users from interacting with content which that user generates, uploads or shares on the service; and (b) reduce the likelihood of that user encountering content which nonverified users generate, upload or share on the service.

These features would: (a) reduce the likelihood of the user encountering “priority content that was harmful to adults”, or particular kinds of such content, by means of the service; or (b) alert the user to the harmful nature of priority content that was harmful to adults that the user may encounter by means of the service. Under clauses 14(6) and (7), Category 1 services would need to have features in place to: (a) prevent non-verified users from interacting with content which that user generates, uploads or shares on the service; and (b) reduce the likelihood of that user encountering content which nonverified users generate, upload or share on the service.

chunks.text: (7), Category 1 services would need to have features in place to: (a) prevent non-verified users from interacting with content which that user generates, uploads or shares on the service; and (b) reduce the likelihood of that user encountering content which nonverified users generate, upload or share on the service. Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 46 to require Category 1 services to enable adult users to see whether other users were verified.76 She said clause 14, as currently drafted, did not truly empower people to make the most wellinformed decisions about the type of content they engaged with. However, amendment 46 would enable people to use information about verification when making judgments about the reliability of other accounts and the content they engaged with. It would also help to disrupt the use of networks of inauthentic accounts to spread disinformation. 77 For the Government, Chris Philp said the effect intended by the amendment was already achieved through clause 14(6) – ie when a user decided to filter out non-verified users, “by definition” such users would be able to see content only from verified users, so they would see from that who was verified and who was not. 78 Kirsty Blackman said the Minister’s argument didn’t “make any sense”: …There is a difference between wanting to filter out everybody who is not verified and wanting to actually see if someone who is threatening someone else online is a verified or a non-verified user. I can understand why a politician, for example, might not want to filter out unverified users but would want to check whether a person was verified before going to the police to report a threat. 79 Chris Philp replied that, regarding police investigations, “if something is illegal and merits a report to the police, users should report it, regardless of whether someone is verified or not - whatever the circumstances”. 80 Amendment 46 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 6. 81 Duties to protect content of democratic importance Clause 15 of the Bill would place duties on Category 1 services to protect “content of democratic importance”. Clause 15(2) would require the use of “proportionate systems and processes designed to ensure that the importance of the free expression of content of democratic importance is taken into account when making decisions about”: (a) how to treat such content (especially decisions about whether to take it down or restrict users’ access to it), and (b) whether to take action against a user generating, uploading or sharing such content.

Kim Leadbeater moved amendment 105 so that the systems and processes would also have to ensure “the safety of people involved in UK elections”. 82 She said the amendment was to protect all political candidates, those working on elections, and those elected to public office at all levels across the UK. It was also to protect our political system. 83 Kim Leadbeater said democracy was under attack each day with “significant volumes of racist, sexist and homophobic hate-filled abuse and threats online to politicians of all parties”. She said this form of abuse harmed society as a whole and deserved specific consideration in the Bill. 84 Chris Philp agreed that abuse and illegal activity directed at anyone, including people running for elected office, was unacceptable.85 However, he pointed out that clause 15 was about stopping social media platforms deleting content through over-zealous takedown. The amendment related to something different - abuse and illegal activities, such as rape threats, that people received on social media, particularly female MPs. Other parts of the Bill delivered what amendment 105 was calling for: First, there are the duties around illegal content that we debated this morning. If either of the above conditions was met, the service provider would have to conduct a children’s risk assessment and the safety duties protecting children would apply. Barbara Keeley moved amendment 22 to remove the “child use” test from the children’s access assessment so that any service likely to be accessed by children would be within scope of the child safety duties. If either of the above conditions was met, the service provider would have to conduct a children’s risk assessment and the safety duties protecting children would apply. Barbara Keeley moved amendment 22 to remove the “child use” test from the children’s access assessment so that any service likely to be accessed by children would be within scope of the child safety duties. The amendment was supported by the NSPCC and other children’s charities. 90 86 87 88 89 90 22 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c275 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c276 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c276 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c276 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c304 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c275 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c276 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c276 Public Bill Committee 7 June 2022 c276 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c304 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Barbara Keeley said children needed protecting wherever they were online. The age-appropriate design code published by the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO), required all services likely to be accessed by children to protect their personal data and privacy. 91 However, the Bill would only regulate user-to-user and search services that had a significant number of child users or services for which children formed a significant part of the user base. It would therefore not apply to all services within scope of the ICO’s code. This would create a “patchwork of regulation that could risk uncertainty, legal battles and unnecessary complexity”. It could also create a “perverse incentive” for online services to delay introducing child safety measures until Ofcom had reached a determination on the categorisation of their sites. 92 Barbara Keeley argued that the children’s access assessment could result in lower standards of protection for children. “Highly problematic” services such as Telegram and OnlyFans could claim they were excluded from the Bill’s child safety duties because children weren’t a significant proportion of their user base. However, evidence had shown children could access those platforms. Other services would also be out of scope of the Bill such as: • blogs promoting life-threatening behaviours, such as pro-anorexia sites with provider-generated rather than user-generated content. • games popular with children that didn’t feature user-generated content but were linked to increasing gambling addiction among children. • other services with user-generated content that was harmful but didn’t affect an appreciable number of children.

The Gambling Act 2005 prohibited children from gambling. chunks.text: the Bill platforms that would not be accessed by children – or had nothing of any concern on them (eg a website about corporation tax) - would not have “unduly burdensome and disproportionate obligation[s] placed on them”. 97 Amendment 22 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 98 Fraudulent advertising Clause 34 of the Bill sets out duties about fraudulent advertising for Category 1 services. Clause 35 sets out fraudulent advertising duties for Category 2A search services. Barbara Keeley explained that, as currently drafted, the duties set out in clauses 34 and 35 would extend to the design, operation and use of a social media platform or search engine service in the UK. Amendments 23 and 24 sought to extend the duties to the design, operation and use of a category 1 or category 2A service that targeted users in the UK. She argued that the amendments would make the Bill more effective by reducing the risk of a company based overseas being able to target UK consumers fraudulently without any action being taken against them: That would be an important change, because paid-for advertisements function by the advertiser stating where in the world, by geographical location, they wish to target consumers. For instance, a company would be able to operate from Hong Kong and take out paid-for advertisements to target consumers just in one particular part of north London. The current wording of the Bill does not acknowledge the fact that internet services can operate from anywhere in the world and use international boundaries to circumvent UK legislation. Other legislation has been successful in tackling scams across borders. I draw the Committee’s attention to the London Olympic Games and Paralympic Games Act 2006, which made it a crime to sell a ticket to the Olympics into the black market anywhere in the world, rather than simply in the UK where the games took place. 99

He said the definitions given at the beginning of the Bill (clause 3(5)(b)) already made it clear that companies would be in scope (both user-to-user and search services) if there were a “significant” number of UK users or where UK users formed one of the target markets or was the only target market. 100 Barbara Keeley challenged the wording of clause 3(5)(b), specifically that a service had links with the UK if it had a significant number of UK users”. 101 She argued that it should not matter if a person is one of 50, 100 or 1,000 people who get scammed by some organisation operating in another country. 102 In 97 98 99 100 101 102 24 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c311 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c313 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c316 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 cc319-20 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c320 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 cc320-1 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c313 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c316 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 cc319-20 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c320 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 cc320-1 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill response, Chris Philip said that where the UK was a target market, there would be no size qualification; the service provider would be in scope, even if there was only one user. 103 Amendment 23 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 104 Amendment 24 was not moved. There was a second division on amendment 44 to clause 36. This amendment sought to add further offences to those which would apply for the purposes of the Bill’s fraudulent advertising provisions. In the Bill, the conditions for an advert to be defined as fraudulent are set out in clause 34(3) for social media platforms (category 1 services) and clause 35(3) for search engines (category 2A services). Both clauses would specify that an advert is fraudulent if it amounts to an offence set out in clause 36. Clause 36 lists a series of offences gathered from financial services legislation and the Fraud Act 2006. Speaking to amendment 44, Barbara Keeley said that charities, such as StepChange and the Money Advice Trust, were concerned that the offences listed in the Bill would not extend to the harm caused by misleading adverts placed by unregulated advertisers, including those that impersonated debt advice charities.105 She said the offences set out in clause 36(2), contained in the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, were designed to address financial market abuse and related to persons unauthorised by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) carrying on an activity that was regulated under that Act. She pointed out that while providing debt counselling and debt adjusting were regulated activities, brokering debt solutions wasn’t. 106 Focusing on clause 36(3) of the Bill, which lists offences under the Fraud Act 2006, Barbara Keeley argued that it would capture harmful online adverts but possibly not unfair and misleading adverts for debt solutions: We therefore have a situation where harmful debt adverts meet the criteria of offence in consumer protection regulations but might not meet the Fraud Act 2006 provisions in the Online Safety Bill. 107

chunks.text: Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill administered by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA). He argued that to introduce this extra offence to the list in clause 36 would create a regulatory overlap and confusion. 109 Amendment 44 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 110 Codes of practice about duties Clause 37 of the Bill would require Ofcom to produce codes of practice setting out guidance on how regulated services would be able to comply with their duties. Clause 37(6) lists some of the bodies that Ofcom would have to consult when preparing draft codes. Schedule 4 sets out the general principles that Ofcom would have to consider when preparing codes of practice, the online safety objectives, and the measures that could be recommended in codes of practice. Amendment 65 was moved by Alex Davies-Jones. This would add organisations campaigning for the removal of animal content to the list of bodies that Ofcom would have to consult. 111 Amendments 59, 63, 64, 66 and 67 were also considered. Amendment 59 would add certain animal welfare offences - under the Animal Welfare Act 2006, the Animal Health and Welfare (Scotland) Act 2006, and the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 – to the list of priority offences in schedule 7. 112 Amendment 63 would amend schedule 4 so that ensuring adequate safeguards to monitor cruelty towards humans and animals would be one of the online safety objectives for user-to-user services. 113 Amendment 64 would amend schedule 4 so that ensuring systems and processes were appropriate to detect cruelty towards humans and animals would be one of the online safety objectives for search services. 114 Amendment 66 would ensure that groups were able to make complaints about animal abuse videos. It would also ensure that dangerous animal abuse content was a reasonable cause for a super-complaint (under clause 140). 115

chunks.text: Committee 9 June 2022 c326 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c327 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c326 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c327 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c327 and c331 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Amendment 67 would make groups campaigning against harmful online content towards humans and animals eligible to make super-complaints. 116 When speaking to amendment 65, Alex Davies-Jones said the Bill was not fit for tackling animal abuse which was “all too common online”. 117 She noted the prevalence of cruelty to animals online had the potential to have a “hugely negative impact” on children who could inadvertently come across such content through social media: 118 …Examples include animals being beaten, set on fire, crushed or partially drowned; the mutilation and live burial of infant monkeys; a kitten intentionally being set on by a dog and another being stepped on and crushed to death; live and conscious octopuses being eaten; and animals being pitted against each other in staged fights. Animals being deliberately placed into frightening or dangerous situations from which they cannot escape or are harmed before being “rescued” on camera is becoming increasingly popular on social media, too. For example, kittens and puppies are “rescued” from the clutches of a python. Such fake rescues not only cause immense suffering to animals, but are fraudulent because viewers are asked to donate towards the rescue and care of the animals. This cannot be allowed to continue. 119

chunks.text: social media, too. For example, kittens and puppies are “rescued” from the clutches of a python. Such fake rescues not only cause immense suffering to animals, but are fraudulent because viewers are asked to donate towards the rescue and care of the animals. This cannot be allowed to continue. 119 In 2020, the RSPCA found there were nearly 500 reports of animal cruelty on social media. 120 Responding for the Government, Chris Philp said some of the offences referred to during the debate were already in the Bill’s scope. For example, content inflicting psychological harm on children was within scope. The Bill also covered extreme pornography. The Minister referred to other policies to protect animals. For example, the maximum sentence for cruelty to animals had been increased from six months to five years in prison. In 2021, the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs announced an action plan for animal welfare, to be delivered through legislative and nonlegislative work. 121 He also said the core intent of the Bill was to protect children, to protect humans from illegal activity, and to stop priority offences. 122 Amendment 65 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 7. 123 Amendment 59 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 124 116 117 118 119 120 121 122 123 124 27 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c327 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c328 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c329 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c328 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c328 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c332 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 cc331-2 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c332 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c396 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: the Bill Amendment 63 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 125 Amendment 64 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 126 Amendment 66 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 127 Amendment 67 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 128 Procedure for issuing codes of practice Under clause 39 of the Bill, Ofcom would have to submit draft codes of practice, prepared under clause 37, to the Secretary of State. These would then be laid before Parliament (unless, under clause 40, the Secretary of State issued any directions to Ofcom). Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 48 requiring Ofcom to prepare draft codes of practice within six months of the passing of the Act. 129 She said that, without a deadline, codes of practice might not come into force for “quite some time” and the “online space needs addressing now”. 130 Platforms and services were concerned at the “ambiguity” of what the Bill would require of them. With a deadline for draft codes of practice, those that wanted to do the right thing could prepare in a timely manner. 131 Chris Philp said various steps were needed before Ofcom could produce codes of practice. For example, secondary legislation would be needed to designate priority categories of content considered harmful to children and content considered harmful to adults. Ofcom would then need to consult before it published the codes. Ofcom had said the process could take twelve months. A six-month deadline might be impossible to meet or make Ofcom “rush and do it in a bad way”. 132 Amendment 48 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 133

Ofcom would then need to consult before it published the codes. Ofcom had said the process could take twelve months. A six-month deadline might be impossible to meet or make Ofcom “rush and do it in a bad way”. 132 Amendment 48 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 7. 133

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c343 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c343 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c344 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 cc346-7 Public Bill Committee 9 June 2022 c348 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill John Nicolson, the Scottish National Party spokesperson for Digital, Culture Media and Sport, moved amendment 84. This would remove the ability of the Secretary of State to modify Ofcom codes of practice for reasons of public policy. 134 When speaking to the amendment, he said the Bill granted the Secretary of State “excessive powers”. Clause 40 had “aggrieved” groups with a broad range of interests, including child safety, human rights, women and girls, sport and democracy, who believed it could threaten the independence of Ofcom. Businesses were also wary of the clause because of the uncertainty it would create. 135 John Nicolson asked the Minister to explain the difference between “public policy” and “government policy” (the wording in the draft Bill) and said Ofcom must not be “politicised”: …Regardless of the political complexion of the Government, when they have too much influence over what people can say online, the implications for freedom of speech are grave, especially when the content that they are regulating is not illegal… 136

chunks.text: the draft Bill) and said Ofcom must not be “politicised”: …Regardless of the political complexion of the Government, when they have too much influence over what people can say online, the implications for freedom of speech are grave, especially when the content that they are regulating is not illegal… 136 Alex Davies-Jones said clause 40 represented a “significant interference in Ofcom’s independence” and the independence of media regulators was the norm in developed democracies. 137 Chris Philp accepted that Ofcom, like other regulators, was “rightly independent and there should be no interference in its day-to-day regulatory decisions”. Clause 40 did not seek to “violate” that principle. However, there might be situations in which a topic, outside of Ofcom’s expertise, needed to be reflected in a code of practice. In those situations, it might be appropriate for a direction to be given to modify a code of conduct. Chris Philp said an example would be an order to reflect the latest medical advice during a public health emergency. 138 The Minister emphasised that the power would only be exercised at the point at which a code was submitted to be laid before Parliament. The power would not allow the Secretary of State to direct Ofcom to require a particular regulated service to take a particular measure. 139 He also pointed out that a revised code would have to be approved by Parliament using the affirmative procedure. This would give parliamentarians the opportunity to respond if they, third parties, or Ofcom had concerns. 140 Amendment 84 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 141 134 135 136 137 138 139 140 141 29

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c355 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c356 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c358 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c357 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c358 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Comments below news articles posted online Clause 49 of the Bill defines “regulated user-generated content” and describes the types of user-generated content that would be exempt from this definition, including “news publisher content”. Kirsty Blackman moved amendment 89. This would remove the exemption for comments below news articles posted online. 142 Alex Davies-Jones’ amendment 43 was also considered. This would remove comments sections operated by news websites where the publisher had a UK turnover of more than £100 million from the exemption for regulated usergenerated content. 143 When speaking to amendment 89, Kirsty Blackman referred to below-the-line comments in newspaper articles as “places that everybody fears to go”, and “worse than Twitter”. She pointed out that comments on a newspaper article can sometimes direct people to even more extreme views. The moderation policies of some newspapers for comments sections were “not strong enough”. Kirsty Blackman said there was no “reasonable excuse” and no logic for them to be excluded from the Bill. 144 Alex Davies-Jones said amendment 43 would ensure that the comments sections of the largest newspaper websites would be subject to the regulatory regime set out in the Bill. She said newspaper comments sections were no different from Facebook and Twitter in that they were social media platforms that allowed users to interact with one another. She noted that Twitter had an estimated 18 million users in the UK, whereas more than twice that number of British people accessed newspaper websites every month, and the harm perpetuated on those platforms was severe. 145 Alex Davies-Jones said the complaints procedure of IPSO (the Independent Press Standards Organisation, the body that many newspapers have joined) was “no solution to the severity of harms that appear in newspaper comments sections”. She also observed that publishers were only legally responsible for illegal material in their comments sections. For everything else, from disinformation to racial prejudice and abuse, regulation through the Bill was needed.146 Chris Philp said the news media publishers had made the case to Government that the comments sections below news articles were an “an integral part of the process of publishing news and of what it means to have a free press”. The Government had accepted that “intruding into that space through legislation and regulation would represent an intrusion into the operation of the free press”. 147 Alex Davies-Jones said the Minister’s argument didn’t

Under clause 52(4), “relevant offence means”: (a) an offence specified in Schedule 5 (terrorism offences), (b) an offence specified in Schedule 6 (offences related to child sexual exploitation and abuse), (c) an offence specified in Schedule 7 (other priority offences), or (d) an offence, not within paragraph (a), (b) or (c), of which the victim or intended victim is an individual (or individuals). Clause 52(7) defines priority illegal content as terrorism content, CSEA content, and content that amounts to an offence listed in schedule 7. Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 61 to bring offences committed overseas within the scope of relevant offences for the purposes of defining illegal content. 152 She noted that animal abuse and cruelty content was often filmed abroad, as was human trafficking content and child sexual exploitation. The Bill would offer “optimal protection” if its definition of illegal content covered matter that would be illegal in either the UK or the country it 148 149 150 151 152 31 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c364 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c365 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c369 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c370 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c377 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c364 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c365 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c369 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c370 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 c377 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill took place in, regardless of whether it originated in the UK. 153 Kirsty Blackman supported the amendment. 154 Chris Philp referred to clause 52(9) of the Bill which states: “For the purposes of determining whether content amounts to an offence, no account is to be taken of whether or not anything done in relation to the content takes place in any part of the United Kingdom.” He said this already did what the amendment was seeking: What that is saying is that it does not matter whether the act of concern takes place physically in the United Kingdom or somewhere else, on the other side of the world. That does not matter in looking at whether something amounts to an offence. If it is criminal under UK law but it happens on the other side of the world, it is still in scope. Clause 52(9) makes that very clear, so I think that that provision is already doing what the shadow Minister’s amendment 61 seeks to do. 155 Alex Davies-Jones claimed her amendment outlined “matters much more explicitly” and left no ambiguity. 156 Amendment 61 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 5. 157

Chris Philp moved Government amendment 116. This would add an offence under section 13 of the Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 1966 - assisting suicide – to the list of offences in schedule 7. Government amendments 117 to 126 were also considered. 158 The Minister explained that these would add “broadly equivalent” offences in Scottish and Northern Irish law to the English and Welsh offences already listed in schedule 7. 159 The Secretary of State would have to consult Scottish Ministers or the Department of Justice in Northern Ireland before making regulations amending schedule 7 in connection with offences extending to Scotland or Northern Ireland only (amendment 126). Clause 54(3) categorises “content that is harmful to adults” as: • priority content harmful to adults (clause 54(3)(a), or • another type of content presenting “a material risk of significant harm to an appreciable number of adults in the UK” (clause 54(3)(b)). Under clause 54(2), the Secretary of State would specify in regulations when content would be priority content harmful to adults. 162 33 Public Bill Committee 14 June 2022 cc394-6 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

This would amend clause 54 so that the Secretary of State’s designation of “priority content that is harmful to adults” would have to include a description of harmful health related misinformation or disinformation (as well as other priority content that might be designated in regulations by the Secretary of State). 163 When speaking to the amendment, John Nicolson said that health-related misinformation and disinformation undermined public health. 164 The COVID-19 pandemic had shown that the consequences of believing false information could be “grave”.165 Alex Davies-Jones said Labour supported the amendment. 166 In his response, Chris Philp referred to the work of the DCMS’ counterdisinformation unit. In some cases, Ministers had engaged directly with social media firms to encourage them to remove inappropriate content. 167 The Minister resisted the amendment, although he agreed with its intention. He said the way to proceed was not to “randomly drop an item into the Bill” – ie harmful health-related disinformation – but set other harmful content through secondary legislation. The Government would continue as planned and designate a list of priority harmful content through a statutory instrument. It was considering a draft list to reassure Members that healthrelated misinformation and disinformation, for example, would be one of the items on it. 168 Amendment 83 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 5. 169

This would amend clause 54 so that the Secretary of State’s designation of “priority content that is harmful to adults” would have to include a description of harmful health related misinformation or disinformation (as well as other priority content that might be designated in regulations by the Secretary of State). 163 When speaking to the amendment, John Nicolson said that health-related misinformation and disinformation undermined public health. 164 The COVID-19 pandemic had shown that the consequences of believing false information could be “grave”.165 Alex Davies-Jones said Labour supported the amendment. 166 In his response, Chris Philp referred to the work of the DCMS’ counterdisinformation unit. In some cases, Ministers had engaged directly with social media firms to encourage them to remove inappropriate content. 167 The Minister resisted the amendment, although he agreed with its intention. He said the way to proceed was not to “randomly drop an item into the Bill” – ie harmful health-related disinformation – but set other harmful content through secondary legislation. The Government would continue as planned and designate a list of priority harmful content through a statutory instrument. It was considering a draft list to reassure Members that healthrelated misinformation and disinformation, for example, would be one of the items on it. 168 Amendment 83 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 5. 169

Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 62. This would require the Secretary of State to consult stakeholders other than Ofcom before making regulations. 170 Alex Davies-Jones said there was a risk of a “potentially dangerous course of events” if there was not a statutory duty on the Secretary of State to consult others when determining how harmful content should be defined. chunks.text: Philp said the Government was already informally working with stakeholders on identifying what the priority harms should be. He was concerned that making this a formal consultation could introduce delays in implementing the Act after Royal Assent. 172 Amendment 62 was negatived on division by 7 votes to 5. 173 Child sexual exploitation and abuse content reporting Clause 59 of the Bill would introduce a new mandatory reporting requirement relating to CSEA content. Under clause 62, it would be a criminal offence if, in purported compliance with a clause 59 requirement, a person knowingly or recklessly provided information that was false in a material respect. Chris Philp moved Government amendment 1 to clause 62. Government amendments 2, 3, 4 and 5 were also considered. The Minister explained these were technical drafting changes in relation to sentencing penalties for eitherway offences in the courts of England and Wales. They would bring the Bill into line with recent changes implemented following the passage of the Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022: The change uses the new term “general limit in a magistrates’ court” to account for any future changes to the sentencing limit in the magistrates court. The 2022 Act includes a secondary power to switch, by regulations, between a 12-month and six-month maximum sentence in the magistrates court, so we need to use the more general language in this Bill to ensure that changes back and forth can be accommodated. If we just fix a number, it would become out of sync if switches are made under the 2022 Act. 174 Amendment 1 was agreed without division. 175

Ofcom would issue guidance on how to comply with the duties (clause 69). When clause 69 was debated, Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 127. 184 This would require Ofcom to publish guidance within six months of the Act being passed to avoid delaying implementation of the provisions “any further than necessary”. 185 Chris Philp was sympathetic to the intent of the amendment. However, he said the Secretary of State should retain the power to determine when to bring Part 5 into force, including the requirement for Ofcom to produce guidance. Ofcom would need “appropriate time and flexibility to get the guidance exactly right” so there were no loopholes that pornography providers might seek to exploit. 186 Amendment 127 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 3. 187 2.3 Part 7 of the Bill: Ofcom’s powers and duties Part 7 of the Bill sets out Ofcom’s duties and powers as the online safety regulator. Chapter 2 would require Ofcom to establish, publish and maintain a register of each regulated service that met the threshold conditions set out in schedule 10 and would be designated as a Category 1, 2A or 2B service. The thresholds for each category would be set out in secondary legislation (subject to the negative procedure) and would relate to a platform’s number of users, its functionalities, and the risk of harm on the platform.

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c443 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc443-4 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c444 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c449 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c449 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill fulfil its aims it needed to be “much stronger on small, very high-risk platforms”: …. They are encouraging and allowing the worst behaviours to thrive on their platforms. They should be held to a higher level of accountability. It is not too much to ask to class them as category 1 platforms. It is not too much to ask them to comply with a higher level of risk assessment requirements and a higher level of oversight from Ofcom. It is not too much to ask because of the massive risk of harm they pose and the massive actual harm that they create. Those platforms should be punished for that. It is one thing to punish and criminalise the behaviour of users on those platforms—individual users create and propagate illegal content or radicalise other users—but the Bill does not go far enough in holding those platforms to account for allowing that to take place. They know that it is happening… 190

She said her view, and that of stakeholders (eg HOPE not hate and the Antisemitism Policy Trust) was that the Government’s approach to categorising services by size rather than harm was “fundamentally flawed”. 191 When replying for the Government, Chris Philp pointed out that all in-scope services, regardless of their user size, would have illegal content duties and duties to protect children. He also stressed that, under schedule 10, the Secretary of State could change the various thresholds, including the size threshold, to bring more companies into scope, if that was considered necessary. 192 Chris Philp also said the Government was “looking carefully” at the problem of small platforms, below the Category 1 threshold, that were “extremely toxic, owing to the way that they are set up, their rules and their user base”. 193 Amendment 80 was negatived on division by 8 votes to 3. 194 Notices to deal with terrorism content or CSEA content Clause 103 of the Bill would give Ofcom the power to require a service provider to use accredited technology to identify and remove terrorism content on public channels and CSEA content on private and public channels. Clause 104 sets out the circumstances under which Ofcom could issue a notice, and the conditions that would have to be met before the power could be used. 190 191 192 193 194 38

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c450 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c451 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c454 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc454-5 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c456 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill When clause 104 was considered, Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 35. This would require Ofcom to consider the presence of relevant content, rather than its prevalence, when considering whether to issue a notice. 195 Amendment 37 was also considered. This would require Ofcom’s risk assessment to consider the risks to adults and children through the production, publication, and dissemination of illegal content. 196 When speaking to amendment 35, Alex Davies-Jones said she welcomed clause 104 but was concerned about the inclusion of prevalence as a factor because of the difficulty in detecting newly produced CSEA content, especially livestreamed abuse. 197 On amendment 37, she said it was important that video content, including livestreaming, was captured by the Bill but was unconvinced that it did so. 198 Chris Philp said he understood the spirit behind amendment 35. However, he said the powers in clause 103 should only be engaged where there was a “reasonable level of risk” – if a single piece of content was present on a platform, it might not be “reasonable or proportionate to force the company to adopt certain new technologies”. The use of “prevalence” would ensure Ofcom’s powers were used where necessary. He also pointed out that the Bill’s provisions on the most harmful illegal content would not place duties on companies to remove “every single piece of illegal content that has ever appeared online” because that would be impossible. They were being asked to take reasonable and proportionate steps to create systems and processes to do so. It was important to frame the legally binding duties in that way to make them realistically achievable. 199 Alex Davies-Jones replied that one image of child sexual exploitation and abuse on a platform was one too many and that the Bill should be as strong as possible.200 Amendment 35 was negatived on division by 5 votes to 3. 201 Amendment 37 was negatived on division by 5 votes to 3. 202 Chris Philp moved Government amendment 6 to clause 104. He explained this was a technical amendment about the drafting of the clause. It was agreed without division. 203
