chunks.text: 200 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc473-4 201 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c474 202 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c474 203 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c474 195 196 39 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Enforcement powers Clause 111 of the Bill lists “enforceable requirements” such as duties to carry out and report on risk assessments, safety duties (including specific duties relating to children) and duties related to users’ rights (freedom of expression and privacy). Failure to comply with the enforceable requirements could trigger enforcement action by Ofcom. When clause 111 was considered Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 53. 204 This was linked to amendment 52 to clause 136 (requiring Ofcom to publish a report about the access independent researchers could have into matters relating to online safety). The amendments would require Ofcom to produce a code of practice on access to data. Alex Davies-Jones said the amendments would make it “abundantly clear” that increased access to data about systems and processes would improve understanding of the online environment. Frances Haugen, the Facebook whistle-blower, had said that expert researchers and academics would need to examine data and look at what was happening behind social media platforms to ensure the Bill was “truly fit for purpose”. 205 Alex Davies-Jones also pointed out that the EU’s Digital Services Act would allow governments, academia and civil society organisations to access companies’ data in order to protect internet users. 206 In response, Chris Philp said researchers’ access to information was covered in clause 136. At present, there was “no concept of independent researchers getting access to the innards of social media companies’ data vaults” so the process had to be set up in a thoughtful and proper way. Ofcom would prepare a report into how that could best be done. This would involve “nontrivial considerations” regarding personal privacy, protecting personal information, who would count as a “valid researcher” and what confidentiality obligations might apply to them. Once Ofcom had reported, it had the power to lay down guidance on providing access for independent researchers to do their work. The process might be “slightly slower” than that proposed through the amendments, but it would “end up in the same place” or a better place.207 Alex Davies-Jones replied that the wording of the Bill was “far too woolly” and again referred to the EU’s Digital Services Act. 208 Amendment 53 was negatived on division by 5 votes to 3. 209
chunks.display_text: 200 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc473-4 201 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c474 202 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c474 203 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c474 195 196 39 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Enforcement powers Clause 111 of the Bill lists “enforceable requirements” such as duties to carry out and report on risk assessments, safety duties (including specific duties relating to children) and duties related to users’ rights (freedom of expression and privacy). Failure to comply with the enforceable requirements could trigger enforcement action by Ofcom. When clause 111 was considered Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 53. 204 This was linked to amendment 52 to clause 136 (requiring Ofcom to publish a report about the access independent researchers could have into matters relating to online safety). The amendments would require Ofcom to produce a code of practice on access to data. Alex Davies-Jones said the amendments would make it “abundantly clear” that increased access to data about systems and processes would improve understanding of the online environment. Frances Haugen, the Facebook whistle-blower, had said that expert researchers and academics would need to examine data and look at what was happening behind social media platforms to ensure the Bill was “truly fit for purpose”. 205 Alex Davies-Jones also pointed out that the EU’s Digital Services Act would allow governments, academia and civil society organisations to access companies’ data in order to protect internet users. 206 In response, Chris Philp said researchers’ access to information was covered in clause 136. At present, there was “no concept of independent researchers getting access to the innards of social media companies’ data vaults” so the process had to be set up in a thoughtful and proper way. Ofcom would prepare a report into how that could best be done. This would involve “nontrivial considerations” regarding personal privacy, protecting personal information, who would count as a “valid researcher” and what confidentiality obligations might apply to them. Once Ofcom had reported, it had the power to lay down guidance on providing access for independent researchers to do their work. The process might be “slightly slower” than that proposed through the amendments, but it would “end up in the same place” or a better place.207 Alex Davies-Jones replied that the wording of the Bill was “far too woolly” and again referred to the EU’s Digital Services Act. 208 Amendment 53 was negatived on division by 5 votes to 3. 209

chunks.text: 206 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c480 207 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc481-2 208 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c482 209 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c482 204 205 40 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Supply chain risk assessments Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 56 to clause 111. The amendment was linked to new clause 11 and would introduce a duty to assess the risk of harm in the supply chain. Alex Davies-Jones pointed out that human content moderation was the foundation of all content moderation for major platforms and was the most important resource for making platforms safe. Human moderators had to look at horrendous and graphic content. However, there was little oversight of their work, and they were employed largely through unaccountable companies, operating at arms-length from the platforms they were supporting. Alex Davies-Jones referred to a Facebook whistleblower from Kenya who had to watch live beheadings and children being sexually exploited. He was paid the equivalent of $2 an hour by the third-party company he worked for. In the US, human moderators were paid roughly $18 an hour. 210 Kirsty Blackman supported the amendment and said she couldn’t see a reason for the Government to reject additional safety for employees who were “doing an incredibly important job that we need them to be doing”. 211 Chris Philp acknowledged the problems referred to by Alex-Davies Jones. However, he said the Bill was not the right place to address them because: •

However, he said the Bill was not the right place to address them because: •

chunks.text: reason for the Government to reject additional safety for employees who were “doing an incredibly important job that we need them to be doing”. 211 Chris Philp acknowledged the problems referred to by Alex-Davies Jones. However, he said the Bill was not the right place to address them because: • UK employment and health and safety laws applied to people employed in the UK. The Bill shouldn’t duplicate or “cut across” those. If the amendment passed, it would apply to people in the UK as much as it would apply to people in Kenya. • the amendment would effectively require Ofcom to start paying regard to employment conditions in Kenya, or potentially any country in the world. This was outside Ofcom’s area of expertise as a communications regulator. • the purpose of the Bill was to keep users safe online, not to regulate working conditions in potentially any country in the world. Alex Davies-Jones replied that the UK was “effectively shipping our trash to other countries” with moderators around the world looking at content created in the UK. There was a duty to protect those people. 212 Amendment 56 was negatived on division by 4 votes to 3. 213 210 211 212 213 41 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc483-4 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c485 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c488 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c488 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: 3. 213 210 211 212 213 41 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 cc483-4 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c485 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c488 Public Bill Committee 16 June 2022 c488 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Penalty notices for non-payment of fees to Ofcom Clause 120 of the Bill would allow Ofcom to impose a financial penalty if a regulated provider did not pay its fee to Ofcom (as required under clause 71). When clause 120 was debated, the Government moved amendments 154 to 157 to ensure that Ofcom would have the power to impose a financial penalty on a regulated provider who failed to pay a fee as required under a new schedule 2 [see below]. 214 When speaking to the amendments, Alex Davies-Jones said they were “crucial in giving Ofcom the important powers it needs to be an effective regulator”. 215 Chris Philp thanked the Shadow Minister for her support. 216 The amendments were agreed without division. 217

When clause 120 was debated, the Government moved amendments 154 to 157 to ensure that Ofcom would have the power to impose a financial penalty on a regulated provider who failed to pay a fee as required under a new schedule 2 [see below]. 214 When speaking to the amendments, Alex Davies-Jones said they were “crucial in giving Ofcom the important powers it needs to be an effective regulator”. 215 Chris Philp thanked the Shadow Minister for her support. 216 The amendments were agreed without division. 217

chunks.text: under a new schedule 2 [see below]. 214 When speaking to the amendments, Alex Davies-Jones said they were “crucial in giving Ofcom the important powers it needs to be an effective regulator”. 215 Chris Philp thanked the Shadow Minister for her support. 216 The amendments were agreed without division. 217 Financial penalties Clause 122 and schedule 12 set out details of the financial penalties that Ofcom would be able to impose on regulated providers. Under paragraph 8 of schedule 12, these would be paid into the Consolidated Fund. When clause 122 was debated, Government amendment 158 was agreed without division. This would mean the requirement to pay penalties to the Consolidated Fund would now be in new clause 43, together with the requirement to pay fees charged under new schedule 2 into the Consolidated Fund. 218 New schedule 2 was introduced through new clause 42. New clause 43 was considered at the same time. Chris Philp explained that the purpose of the grouping was to allow Ofcom to recover its initial costs: New schedule 2, which is quite detailed, makes provisions in connection with Ofcom’s ability to recover its initial costs, which we have previously debated. As discussed, it is important that the taxpayer not only is protected from the ongoing costs but that the set-up costs are recovered. The taxpayer should not have to pay for the regulatory framework; the people who are being regulated should pay, whether the costs are incurred before or after commencement, in line with the “polluter pays” principle… 219 Alex Davies-Jones welcomed the provisions of new schedule 2. 220 New clauses 42 and 43 were added to the Bill without division. 221 New schedule 2 was added to the Bill. 222

chunks.text: 219 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c604 220 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c604 221 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c605 222 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c698 214 215 42 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Business disruption measures Ofcom would be able to apply to the courts for “business disruption measures”- court orders requiring third parties to withdraw services or block access to non-compliant regulated services. They would be used for the “most serious instances of user harm”. 223 Service restriction orders (clause 123) would require the providers of “ancillary services” to take steps to disrupt the business or revenue of a noncompliant provider’s operations. Ancillary services would include companies providing payment or advertising services. Access restriction orders (clause 125) would require internet services providers and app stores (for example) to take steps to prevent, restrict, or deter UK users from accessing a non-compliant service. When clause 123 was considered, Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 50. This would enable Ofcom to act against a schedule of non-compliant sites, while preserving the right of those sites to oppose the application for, and/or appeal through the courts against any orders to block access or support services. 224 Amendment 51 to clause 125 was also considered. This would give Ofcom the ability to act against a schedule of non-compliant sites, while preserving the right of those sites to oppose an application for an order to block access or support services, or to appeal through the courts against any such order. 225 Alex Davies-Jones said the Bill’s business disruption measures would be “ineffective” if Ofcom had to apply for separate court orders when trying to “protect people across the board from the same harms”: …The Bill currently requires Ofcom to seek a separate court order for each service against which it wishes to take enforcement action in the form of blocking access or services. That is the only effective mechanism for overseas websites. UK-based services will be subject to enforcement notices and financial penalties that can be enforced without having to go to court. That creates a disadvantage for UK sites, which can be more easily enforced against. Given that there are 4 million to 5 million pornographic websites, for example, the requirement for separate court orders will prevent Ofcom from taking action at scale and creating a level playing field for all adult sites. Under the Bill, Ofcom must take action against each offending website or social media company individually. While we acknowledge that the Government have stated that enforcement action can be taken against multiple offending content providers, in our opinion that is not made clear in the Bill. 226

That is why Ofcom must be granted clear enforcement powers to take swift action against multiple websites and content providers in one court action or order. 227 Kirsty Blackman supported the amendments. 228 For the Government, Chris Philp said he recognised the intent behind the amendments ie for Ofcom to be able to make bulk applications to the court if necessary. However, he pointed out that the civil procedure rules already allowed multi-party claims to be made. Under the rules, Ofcom would be able to make bulk applications to deal with very large numbers of non-compliant websites and service providers in one go. 229 Alex-Davies-Jones welcomed the Minister’s clarification of the rules, but said she wanted to remove any ambiguity and put the “important measures” in the Bill. 230 Amendment 50 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 231 Amendment 51 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 232

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c503 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c503 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c503 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc504-5 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c505 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill clause 129 involved the Secretary of State “far too closely in the enforcement of the regime” and she could therefore not support the clause. 235 Chris Philp said clause 129(4) set out an obligation to consult and would give the Secretary of State an opportunity to offer an opinion. However, this would not be binding on Ofcom. He therefore disagreed that it represented an “inappropriate interference” in the operation of a regulator. 236 Kirsty Blackman welcomed amendment 7 and said she didn’t have a problem with clause 129 requiring the Secretary of State to be consulted. 237 Amendment 7 was agreed on division by 10 votes to 4. 238 Committees Clause 130 of the Bill would require Ofcom to set up an advisory committee on disinformation and misinformation. The Explanatory Notes state this is “because the spread of inaccurate information, regardless of intent, is particularly concerning”. 239 The Committee would provide advice to Ofcom on: • how the providers of regulated services should deal with disinformation and misinformation. • how Ofcom should exercise its power under clause 64 (on transparency reporting) relating to disinformation and misinformation. • how Ofcom should exercise its duty to promote media literacy in relation to countering disinformation and misinformation on regulated services.

chunks.text: 2022 c506 239 Explanatory Notes to the Online Safety Bill (PDF), 10 May 2022, para 586 240 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c506 241 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c506 235 236 45 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill “identifiable powers or active role in tackling harmful misinformation and disinformation”. According to Alex Davies-Jones, the committee’s remit needed strengthening and clarifying, so that it could more effectively tackle harmful disinformation and misinformation. This should include advising on Ofcom’s research, reporting on drivers of harmful misinformation and disinformation, and proportionate responses to them. There should also be a duty on Ofcom to consult the committee when drafting relevant codes of practice. Amendment 57 was a “simple amendment” to “encourage scrutiny”. 242 Alex Davies-Jones said it seemed an “obvious omission” for the Bill not to provide for a specific code of practice around reducing disinformation. Amendment 58 would actively encourage services to reduce disinformation across their platforms. 243 Kirsty Blackman said the Bill dealt with misinformation and disinformation in a “wishy-washy way”. She supported Labour’s amendments. 244 For the Government, Chris Philp referred to the role of the DCMS’ counterdisinformation unit, to the new foreign interference offence in the National Security Bill, currently before Parliament. 245 Once that Bill had passed, the offence could be added to schedule 7 of the online safety legislation as a priority offence. The Minister also noted that certain kinds of disinformation and misinformation that harmed adults could be designated as a priority category of harm. 246 Amendment 57 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 247 Amendment 58 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 5. 248 2.4

According to Alex Davies-Jones, the committee’s remit needed strengthening and clarifying, so that it could more effectively tackle harmful disinformation and misinformation. This should include advising on Ofcom’s research, reporting on drivers of harmful misinformation and disinformation, and proportionate responses to them. Amendment 57 was a “simple amendment” to “encourage scrutiny”. 242 Alex Davies-Jones said it seemed an “obvious omission” for the Bill not to provide for a specific code of practice around reducing disinformation. chunks.text: October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill provider of one or more regulated services would only be admissible if Ofcom considered that the complaint was: • of “particular importance”, or • impacted on a “particularly large number” of users of the service or members of the public. Kirsty Blackman moved amendment 77 to remove subclause (2). 249 She said the subclause didn’t “make sense when we are considering the Facebooks of this world”: …If someone is making a complaint about a single regulated service and that service is Facebook, Twitter, TikTok or another large platform—or a new, yetto-be-created platform—that significant numbers of people use, there is no justification for treating that complaint differently just because it is against a single entity. When a complaint is made against Facebook—I am picking on Facebook because 85% of the UK public are members of it; it is an absolute behemoth—I would like there to be no delay in its being taken to Ofcom. I would like Ofcom not to have to check and justify that the complaint is “of particular importance”. 250

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c523 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c524 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c525 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c525 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc527-8 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Kirsty Blackman moved amendment 153 so that Ofcom would have to respond to complaints within 90 days. She argued it was important for the Bill to have a timeframe for super-complaints for several reasons: • third sector organisations bringing super-complaints would have limited budgets. If there was an unnecessary delay before Ofcom decided whether a complaint was eligible, charities could spend money unnecessarily on building up a further case for the next stages of the complaint. • it would help Ofcom when setting and levying fees. • in some cases - eg child sexual abuse images - cases would need to be dealt with very quickly, possibly in a matter of hours or days. 256 Barbara Keeley supported the amendment, claiming there had to be a strict deadline for Ofcom to respond to super-complaints for the system to be meaningful. 257 Chris Philp resisted the amendment. He argued that time limits should be set out in regulations (made under clause 141) rather than in the Bill. Ofcom and other “appropriate bodies” would be consulted to ensure deadlines were “realistic and reasonable”. 258 The Minister also pointed out that it might not be possible to deal with complicated or difficult cases within an overall time limit (eg 90 days). 259 Kirsty Blackman appreciated the Minister’s comments but maintained there should be a time limit in the Bill. 260 Amendment 153 was negatived on division by 10 votes to 5. 261 2.5

chunks.text: 259 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c536 260 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c537 261 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c537 262 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc545-6 256 257 48 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill In response, Chris Philp said the Government was introducing a “new, groundbreaking regime” and was trying to strike a balance between the regulatory independence of Ofcom and “appropriate roles for Parliament and Government”. The guidance issued under clause 147 would be strategic and would not stray into operational or organisational matters that should rest exclusively with Ofcom. The Minister also noted that, under clause 147(8), Ofcom “must have regard to the guidance”. This was different from a “hardedged statutory obligation” to follow the guidance in full. 263 The Committee divided on whether clause 147 stand part of the Bill. This was agreed by 10 votes to 5. 264 2.6

chunks.text: also noted that, under clause 147(8), Ofcom “must have regard to the guidance”. This was different from a “hardedged statutory obligation” to follow the guidance in full. 263 The Committee divided on whether clause 147 stand part of the Bill. This was agreed by 10 votes to 5. 264 2.6 Part 10 of the Bill: communications offences Part 10 of the Bill would introduce three new criminal offences involving harmful, false or threatening communications, and a new offence of sending a photograph or film of a person’s genitals to another person (commonly known as ‘cyberflashing’). The new offences are based on recommendations made by the Law Commission following a review of the law on abusive and offensive online communications. 265 There were three divisions on Part 10 of the Bill. The Committee considered two amendments related to ‘epilepsy trolling’, both of which were negatived on division. Chris Philp did, however, confirm that the Government intends to legislate on the issue and that he will provide further details at Report. The Committee also considered an amendment relating to cyberflashing and consent, which again was negatived on division. However, Chris Philp undertook to look at the drafting of this provision to see if it could be further improved. The Committee agreed two technical drafting amendments to the sentencing provisions in clause 156. These are not considered further in this briefing. chunks.text: as a consequence of receiving the content of a message sent online Amendment 113, tabled by Paul Maynard (Conservative), would have added the following wording: physical harm resulting from an epileptic seizure, where the seizure has been triggered by the intentional sending of flashing images to a person with epilepsy Both amendments related to the issue of ‘epilepsy trolling’, where flashing images are sent to people with photosensitive epilepsy with the aim of triggering a seizure. In written evidence to the Committee, the Epilepsy Society noted that the harm caused in such cases “is often of a physical nature”. 267 The Law Commission had recommended the introduction of a specific offence to cover flashing images being sent maliciously to known sufferers of epilepsy as part of its review of the existing law on communications offences, but did not make any recommendations as to the precise form of such an offence. 268 In February 2022, the Government said that it was continuing to assess this proposal and would issue a full response to the Law Commission later in 2022. 269 Speaking to the amendments, Kim Leadbeater said that limiting the definition of “harm” to psychological harm failed to accurately and fully reflect the harm caused by epilepsy trolling, and risked leaving “a significant loophole”: For many people with epilepsy, the harm caused by this vicious online trolling is not only psychological but physical too. Seizures are not benign events. They can result in broken bones, concussion, bruises and cuts, and in extreme cases can be fatal. It is simply not right to argue that physical harm is intrinsically

They are different harms with different symptoms. While victims may experience both, that is not always the case. 270 She said that expanding the definition to cover physical harm would give full effect to ‘Zach’s law’, a campaign for a standalone epilepsy trolling law. The campaign is named for 11-year-old Zach Eagling, who has cerebral palsy and epilepsy and was targeted by online trolls when he did a sponsored walk for the Epilepsy Society.271 She noted that a proposal for a standalone Bill was “under discussion in the Ministry of Justice”, but argued there was no need to delay action as the Online Safety Bill was “a perfectly fit legislative vehicle to end epilepsy trolling”. 272 In response, Chris Philp confirmed that “the Government have decided to enact the Law Commission’s recommendations” to introduce “a new and separate standalone offence that is specific to epilepsy for the very first time”.273 He said that he was in the process of getting cross-Government agreement on which legislative vehicle to use, and that he expected to make a further announcement on Report as to which Bill the new offence will appear in. He acknowledged Members’ wishes to act quickly but resisted amendments 112 and 113 based on a number of drafting issues. He said that amendment 112 covered physical harm in general and therefore risked creating “duplicative criminal law” by overlapping with existing offences such as assisting suicide. 274 He added that while amendment 113 specifically mentioned epilepsy, more detailed drafting was required to cover issues such what level of harm should be covered, what the penalty should be, and how terms such as “epilepsy” and “epileptic seizures” should be defined. Kim Leadbeater pressed both amendments to a division. Each was defeated by 10 votes to 5. 275

The offence would only be committed where: • 270 271 272 273 274 275 51 the sender intended that the recipient would see the genitals and be caused alarm, distress or humiliation, or Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c549 Epilepsy Society website, #ZachsLaw: world leading legislation (accessed 31 October 2023) Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c550 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c552 Section 2(1) of the Suicide Act 1961 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc556-7 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill • the sender’s purpose was to obtain sexual gratification and they were reckless as to whether the recipient would be caused alarm, distress or humiliation.

chunks.text: Suicide Act 1961 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc556-7 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill • the sender’s purpose was to obtain sexual gratification and they were reckless as to whether the recipient would be caused alarm, distress or humiliation. The proposed offence is based on the Law Commission’s recommendation for a new ‘cyberflashing’ offence. The Law Commission considered that including a requirement for the sender to have a specific intent or motivation – rather than basing the offence on whether the recipient had consented – would avoid the risk of ‘over-criminalising’ the conduct the new offence aims to tackle: We have real sympathy with the argument that an absence of consent is common to all acts of cyberflashing (ie those communicated images we would want to criminalise). However, we are not persuaded that the touchstone of criminal wrongfulness lies in the absence of consent alone. The threshold of criminality for this sort of conduct must be higher than that. 276 At Committee Stage Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 41, which would have expanded the clause 156 offence to include cases where the recipient had not consented for the sender to share the photograph or film with them. Alex Davies-Jones said that Labour welcomed clause 156 but that the Government had “missed a huge opportunity to accurately capture the problems caused by sharing intimate images online”. 277 She argued that “cyber-flashing is problematic because it is non-consensual conduct of a sexual nature”: Distributing these images is not in and of itself wrong, but doing so without the consent of the recipient is. The non-consensual act breaches women’s rights to sexual autonomy, to be treated with dignity and to be free from sexual violence, regardless of the motive of the perpetrator. 278
chunks.display_text: Suicide Act 1961 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc556-7 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill • the sender’s purpose was to obtain sexual gratification and they were reckless as to whether the recipient would be caused alarm, distress or humiliation. The proposed offence is based on the Law Commission’s recommendation for a new ‘cyberflashing’ offence. The Law Commission considered that including a requirement for the sender to have a specific intent or motivation – rather than basing the offence on whether the recipient had consented – would avoid the risk of ‘over-criminalising’ the conduct the new offence aims to tackle: We have real sympathy with the argument that an absence of consent is common to all acts of cyberflashing (ie those communicated images we would want to criminalise). However, we are not persuaded that the touchstone of criminal wrongfulness lies in the absence of consent alone. The threshold of criminality for this sort of conduct must be higher than that. 276 At Committee Stage Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 41, which would have expanded the clause 156 offence to include cases where the recipient had not consented for the sender to share the photograph or film with them. Alex Davies-Jones said that Labour welcomed clause 156 but that the Government had “missed a huge opportunity to accurately capture the problems caused by sharing intimate images online”. 277 She argued that “cyber-flashing is problematic because it is non-consensual conduct of a sexual nature”: Distributing these images is not in and of itself wrong, but doing so without the consent of the recipient is. The non-consensual act breaches women’s rights to sexual autonomy, to be treated with dignity and to be free from sexual violence, regardless of the motive of the perpetrator. 278

chunks.text: nature”: Distributing these images is not in and of itself wrong, but doing so without the consent of the recipient is. The non-consensual act breaches women’s rights to sexual autonomy, to be treated with dignity and to be free from sexual violence, regardless of the motive of the perpetrator. 278 She argued that the existing drafting of clause 156 as a “motive-based offence” was too limited and would make it difficult to investigate and prosecute the new offence: Proving a motive behind an offence as simple as merely sending a photograph is nigh on impossible. If we really want to see systemic change in attitudes to women and girls, we fundamentally should not be creating laws that place the burden on the victim. A consent-based offence, as in our amendments, covers all forms of cyber-flashing, regardless of the motives of the sender. Motive requirements create an unjustified hierarchy of abuses and victims, and they do not reflect victims’ experiences. Requiring proof of specific motives will make investigations and prosecutions more difficult. 279 276 277 278 279 52 Law Commission, Modernising Communications Offences: A final report (PDF), Law Com No 399, July 2021, para 6.108 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c561 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c561 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c562 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Communications Offences: A final report (PDF), Law Com No 399, July 2021, para 6.108 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c561 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c561 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c562 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill She considered that a consent-based offence would provide “a much better foundation for education and prevention projects” and would send the message that “all sexual activity should be grounded in consent”. 280 Dame Maria Miller said that she understood why the Law Commission had decided against recommending a consent-based offence. As an alternative she suggested that the clause’s current “tie” between obtaining sexual gratification and being reckless could be removed, which would capture senders who were reckless as to whether the recipient would be caused alarm, distress or humiliation without also requiring them to have had the purpose of sexual gratification. 281 In response, Chris Philp said he would resist Labour’s amendment for the time being. However, he committed to considering whether the drafting of clause 156 could be improved further, particularly looking at the recklessness point made by Maria Miller. Alex Davies-Jones pressed amendment 41 to a division. It was defeated by 9 votes to 5. 282 2.7 Part 11 of the Bill: supplementary and general provisions Powers to amend schedules 5, 6 and 7 Clause 176 of the Bill would give the Secretary of State powers to amend schedules 4, 6 and 7 of the Bill. Government amendment 126 was agreed without debate. 283 This would require the Secretary of State to consult Scottish Ministers or the Department of Justice in Northern Ireland before making regulations amending schedule 7 in connection with an offence which extended to Scotland or Northern Ireland only.

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c562 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 cc564-5 Public Bill Committee 21 June 2022 c569 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c588 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 cc589-90 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Interpretation Clause 189(1) of the Bill defines content as “anything communicated by means of an internet service, whether publicly or privately, including written material or messages, oral communications, photographs, videos, visual images, music and data of any description”. Kirsty Blackman moved amendment 76 so that anything communicated by means of an internet service would be considered content, not only the examples listed in clause 189(1). She said this was important to future-proof the Bill – even though the current definition of content said “including”, this could be “unnecessarily restrictive and could be open to challenge should someone invent something that is not on the list”. 285 Alex Davies-Jones supported the amendment, also claiming it was important for future-proofing the Bill. 286 Chris Philp said he understood the point of the amendment and agreed that the Bill should cover matters not listed in clause 189(1). However, he argued that the word “including” didn’t limit content to the types then referred to in the clause. The definition was therefore not restrictive.287 Amendment 76 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 6. 288

The definition was therefore not restrictive.287 Amendment 76 was negatived on division by 9 votes to 6. 288 Extent The Obscene Publications Act 1959 applies in England and Wales. The Act makes it an offence to publish an obscene article. When clause 192 (extent) was considered, Chris Philp moved Government amendment 141. The Government’s new clause 35 was also considered. The Minister explained that new clause 35, to be inserted after clause 171, would create a defence for Ofcom to the offence of publishing an obscene article where Ofcom was exercising its online safety duties. Amendment 141 would mean that new clause 35 would extend only to England and Wales. 289 Alex Davies-Jones supported the amendment, as did Kirsty Blackman, and it was agreed without division. 290 New clause 35 was added to the Bill. 291 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c593 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 cc593-4 287 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c594 288 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c597 289 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c598 290 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 cc598-9 291 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c603 285 286 54 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: 288 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c597 289 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c598 290 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 cc598-9 291 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c603 285 286 54 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Commencement When clause 193 (commencement) was considered, Alex Davies-Jones moved amendment 49. This would bring Part 5 of the Bill (duties relating to pornographic content) into force three months after the Bill passed.292 Alex Davies-Jones said the amendment would bring “real, meaningful change as soon as is practically possible”. She noted that measures to protect children from online pornography (initially through Part 3 of the Digital Economy Act 2017) had not happened and that her amendment would bring “positive change”. 293 Kirsty Blackman supported the amendment. 294 For the Government, Chris Philp said an important consideration for the commencement of Part 5 was the need to ensure that all kinds of providers of online pornography were treated the same, including those hosting usergenerated content, which were subject to the duties of Part 3: …If we take a piecemeal approach, bringing into force part 5, on commercial pornography, before part 3, on user-to-user pornography, that may enable some of the services, which are quite devious, to simply reconfigure their services to circumvent regulation or cease to be categorised as part 5 services and try to be categorised as part 3 services. We want to do this in a comprehensive way to ensure that no one will be able to wriggle out of the provisions in the Bill. 295

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c602 296 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c602 297 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c602 298 Public Bill Committee 23 June 2022 c603 292 293 55 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 2.8 Other issues on which the Committee divided Advocacy Body for child users Barbara Keeley moved new clause 3. This would create an “Advocacy Body” to represent the interests of child users of regulated services.299 Barbara Keeley argued that a strong, authoritative, and well-resourced voice that spoke for children would ensure that complex safeguarding issues were well understood and would inform Ofcom’s decisions. Charities had highlighted that the complaints and reporting mechanisms in the Bill might not always be appropriate for children. Barbara Keeley said a children’s advocacy body would be able to support children with redress mechanisms that were “fundamentally targeted at adults”. Given how many children use the internet, this was “an essential element that is missing from the Bill”. 300 Chris Philp referred to the Bill’s “extremely strong” protections for children. 301 He also pointed out that clause 17(6)(c) would allow a parent (or other adult with responsibility) for a child to raise content-reporting claims with users, so that children were not left on their own. Moreover, the complaints procedures set out in clause 18(2)(c) would have to be “easy to access, easy to use (including by children)”. The Minister said there already was a statutory organisation speaking for children ie the Children’s Commissioner for England, with equivalents for Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland. 302 Kim Leadbeater responded that the Children’s Commissioner for England was calling for an advocacy body. 303 Chris Philp said if new clause 3 was agreed, there would be a second statutory advocate or body with duties that would be the same as some of those that the Children’s Commissioner already exercised. Alex Davies-Jones pointed out there were different Children’s Commissioners across the nations of the UK: “Each would have the same rights to advocate for children, so we would have four, rather than one focusing on one specific issue, which is what the Children’s Commissioners across the UK are advocating for”. 304 The Committee voted against new clause 3 by 9 votes to 6. 305 Disclosure of information to Ofcom Barbara Keeley moved new clause 4. This would require regulated companies to proactively disclose information to Ofcom on material changes to their

chunks.text: 309 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c625 310 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c626 311 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c627 312 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 cc628-9 306 307 57 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill services – eg if a risk assessment identified weaknesses or vulnerabilities in a service. 313 The Committee voted against new clause 9 by 9 votes to 6. 314 Ofcom powers New clause 10, in the name of Alex Davies-Jones, would give Ofcom the power to take steps where it considered there was a threat to the health and safety of the public or to national security, without the need for a direction from the Secretary of State. The Committee voted by 9 votes to 6, without debate, against giving the new clause a second reading.315 Secretary of State’s powers to direct New clause 12, in the name of Alex Davies-Jones, would give the Secretary of State powers to suggest modifications to a code of practice, as opposed to the powers of direction set out in clause 40. The Committee voted by 9 votes to 6, without debate, against giving the new clause a second reading.316 Liability for third party breaches of safety duties New clause 13 would impose liability on a provider where a company providing regulated services on its behalf failed to comply with the duties in the Bill. Alex Davies-Jones explained the purpose of the new clause: …Labour fears a scenario in which a company captured by the regulatory regime established by the Bill will argue that an element of its service is not within the ambit of the regulator simply because it is part of a supply chain, represented by, but not necessarily the responsibility of, the regulated services. The contracted element, supported by an entirely separate company, would argue that it is providing business-to-business services. That is not user-touser generated content per se but content designed and delivered at arm’s length, provided to the user-to-user service to deploy to its users. The result would likely be a timely, costly and unhelpful legal process during which systems could not be effectively regulated. The same may apply in relation to moderators, where complex contract law would need to be invoked. 317

The Committee voted by 9 votes to 6, without debate, against giving the new clause a second reading.315 Secretary of State’s powers to direct New clause 12, in the name of Alex Davies-Jones, would give the Secretary of State powers to suggest modifications to a code of practice, as opposed to the powers of direction set out in clause 40. The Committee voted by 9 votes to 6, without debate, against giving the new clause a second reading.316 Liability for third party breaches of safety duties New clause 13 would impose liability on a provider where a company providing regulated services on its behalf failed to comply with the duties in the Bill. Alex Davies-Jones explained the purpose of the new clause: …Labour fears a scenario in which a company captured by the regulatory regime established by the Bill will argue that an element of its service is not within the ambit of the regulator simply because it is part of a supply chain, represented by, but not necessarily the responsibility of, the regulated services. The contracted element, supported by an entirely separate company, would argue that it is providing business-to-business services. That is not user-touser generated content per se but content designed and delivered at arm’s length, provided to the user-to-user service to deploy to its users. The result would likely be a timely, costly and unhelpful legal process during which systems could not be effectively regulated. The same may apply in relation to moderators, where complex contract law would need to be invoked. 317

chunks.text: arm’s length, provided to the user-to-user service to deploy to its users. The result would likely be a timely, costly and unhelpful legal process during which systems could not be effectively regulated. The same may apply in relation to moderators, where complex contract law would need to be invoked. 317 Chris Philp said the new clause would “impose an unreasonable burden on businesses and cause confusion over which companies are required to comply with the duties in the Bill”. He argued that the Bill ensured “legal certainty and clarity” over which companies were subject to duties. Clause 180 made it clear that the Bill’s duties would fall on companies with control over the regulated service – these companies would be responsible for ensuring that any third parties (eg contractors) were complying with the Bill’s safety duties. New clause 15 would require Ofcom to publish a strategy related to its duty to promote media literacy.321 New clause 16, contingent on new clause 15, would require Ofcom to publish a progress report on its media literacy strategy. 322 Alex Davies-Jones noted that the draft Online Safety Bill contained a new media duty for Ofcom (to replace the one in section 11 of the Communications Act 2003). This had been dropped from the final Bill. Media literacy initiatives were now only mentioned in the context of risk assessments. There was no “active requirement” for internet companies to promote media literacy. New clauses 14, 15 and 16 would introduce a new, stronger media literacy duty on Ofcom, with specific objectives. 323 Kirsty Blackman supported the “vital” new clauses. 324 For the Government, Chris Philp said the duty proposed in new clause 14 was narrower than the existing duty in the 2003 Act. He would therefore resist the new clause. 325 The Committee voted against new clause 14 by 9 votes to 6. 326 Algorithmic prompts: prohibition of protected characteristics New clause 17 would remove the ability of search services to allow their algorithms to create prompts in relation to protected characteristics. This would remove the possibility that a prompt would contain discriminatory language toward an individual or group with protected characteristics. When speaking to the new clause, Kirsty Blackman illustrated its purpose by pointing out that if someone were to type “Jews are” in a search service, the results obtained through algorithmic prompts could be “overwhelmingly racist, overwhelmingly antisemitic, overwhelmingly discriminatory”. She said the new clause would mean that if someone started to type something about

He also said the new clause could have the unintended consequence of banning any reference in prompts to protected characteristics, although people looking for help or support might find such prompts helpful. 328 The Committee voted against new clause 17 by 9 votes to 6. 329 Identification of information incidents by Ofcom New clause 18 would give Ofcom a proactive role in identifying and responding to information incidents that can occur in times of crisis. 330 When speaking to the new clause, Kirsty Blackman said the Bill was “focused on regulating the day-to-day online environment” and that “information incidents” (eg during a terror attack or pandemic) were not dealt with effectively enough: …The new clause might involve Ofcom introducing a system whereby emerging incidents could be reported publicly and different actors could request the regulator to convene a response group. The provision would allow Ofcom to be more proactive in its approach and…to provide clear guidance… 331

When speaking to the new clause, Barbara Keeley said that power was “currently skewed significantly against individuals and in favour of big corporations” with people feeling that companies took no action in response to complaints. 342 In response, Chris Philp said an individual could already seek redress through the courts where a company caused them to suffer loss through negligence or some other behaviour giving rise to grounds for civil liability. This could include a breach of a company’s terms of service. He also said that a more effective way of dealing with problems was for Ofcom to deal with them on behalf of the public on a systemic basis. 343 The Committee voted against new clause 24 by 9 votes to 6. 344

chunks.text: 348 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c660 349 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c668 350 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c669 351 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c670 345 346 62 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Chris Philip pointed out that all companies, regardless of the number of child users, would have duties to address illegal content affecting children (eg CSEA content and illegal suicide content). He also said that new clause 27 would introduce the possibility that, even when there were no children on a service, and no children were ever likely to use it, the Bill’s safety duties would be engaged. This raised issues of proportionality, and, on this basis, he would resist the clause. However, the Minister did say the Government was reflecting “very carefully” on services with a small number of users that posed a high risk of harm. 352 The Committee voted against new clause 27 by 9 votes to 7. 353 Empowerment features for child users New clause 28 would enable Ofcom to require services to provide empowerment features for child users. Kirsty Blackman said the new clause was an attempt to address the Bill’s asymmetry in relation to the lack of user empowerment features for child users. The new clause would mean that, if a service was required to have user empowerment features in place for adults, then Ofcom could require it to provide equivalent features designed specifically for child users. 354 For Labour, Barbara Keeley supported the new clause. 355 For the Government, Chris Philp explained, that user empowerment had been applied to adults but not children, because children were already protected “a lot more than adults” through the Bill’s child risk assessment duties and child safety duties. He said these strong protections meant children didn’t need to benefit from the Bill’s user empowerment duties. 356 The Committee voted against new clause 28 by 9 votes to 7. 357 Accessibility to adult users with learning disabilities New clause 29 would require complaints, user empowerment and user reporting functions to be accessible and understandable to adult users with learning disabilities. 358 When speaking to the new clause, Kirsty Blackman said Mencap had raised the issue of accessibility for people with learning disabilities and who had a “right to access the internet just as we all do”. 359 Barbara Keeley supported the new clause.360 Chris Philp said the Bill as drafted would already meet the objectives of new clause 29. He also noted that Ofcom was subject to the public sector equality

The Committee voted by 9 votes to 7, without debate, against giving the clause a second reading. 363 Pornography Part 5 of the Bill covers non-user-generated pornography (ie commercial pornography sites) and would require providers to ensure that children were “not normally able to encounter” this content (for example, by using age verification). User-generated pornography would be covered by the safety duties set out in Part 3 of the Bill. John Nicolson tabled three new clauses on pornography. New clause 38 would apply adults’ risk assessment duties to pornographic sites. 364 New clause 39 would apply safety duties protecting adults to regulated provider pornographic content. New clause 40 would apply duties to prevent users from encountering illegal content to regulated providers of pornographic content. 365 When speaking to the new clauses, John Nicolson explained how the global online pornography industry was a “proven driver of big harms”: … It causes the spread of image-based sexual abuse and child sexual abuse material. It normalises sexual violence and harmful sexual attitudes and behaviours, and it offers children easy access to violent, sexist and racist sexual content, which is proven to cause them a whole range of harms…

chunks.text: 365 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 cc685-6 366 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c686 367 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c686 368 Public Bill Committee 28 June 2022 c687 361 362 64 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill For the Government, Chris Philp explained the Bill’s approach to pornography: user-to-user sites would be “comprehensively covered” by the safety duties in Part 3. The duties in Part 5 were “narrowly targeted at protecting children in the commercial part of the market” (which had been “wrongly omitted” from the draft Bill). 369 The Minister further explained: …A much wider range of duties is placed by part 3 on the user-to-user part of the pornography market. The user-to-user services covered by part 3 are likely to include the largest sites with the least control; as the content is user generated, there is no organising mind—whatever gets put up, gets put up. It is worth drawing the distinction between the services covered in part 3 and part 5 of the Bill. In relation to part 5 services publishing their own material, Parliament can legislate, if it chooses to, to make some of that content illegal, as it has done in some areas—some forms of extreme pornography are illegal. If Parliament thinks that the line is drawn in the wrong place and need to be moved, it can legislate to move that line as part of the general legislation in this area. 370 The Committee voted against new clause 38 by 9 votes to 6. 371 The Committee voted against new clause 39 by 9 votes to 6. 372 The Committee voted against new clause 40 by 9 votes to 6. 373 The Bill [Bill 121 2022/23](PDF), as amended in Committee, was published.

Update for report stage In one Written Ministerial Statement, Nadine Dorries, then Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport, explained that Government amendments had been tabled to the Bill. These would, among other things: 66 • make changes to the illegal safety duties to address concerns about cross-platform harms and breadcrumbing. • give Ofcom further powers to require companies to take additional steps to tackle child sexual exploitation and abuse online. • strengthen the harmful and false communications offences. • make clear that Category 1 service providers could decide to allow harmful content on their services. • ensure that recognised news publishers’ content remained online while an appeal took place. • introduce a new duty for Ofcom to identify and publish a list of companies close to the Category 1 threshold. •

This would capture “covert attempts by foreign state actors to manipulate our information environment in order to interfere in UK society and undermine our democratic, political and legal processes”. 375

The final details of the types of content covered by the three categories would be designated in secondary legislation following consultation with Ofcom. 377 3.2 Government amendments to the Bill The first day of report stage took place on 12 July 2022. Government amendments were added to the Bill relating to journalistic content, adult safety duties, and illegal content duties. Protecting journalistic content Content published on a newspaper or broadcaster’s website is not in scope of the Bill and user comments on that content are exempt. However, journalistic content on Category 1 services is in scope. 376 377 68 DCMS, Online Safety Bill: Scope, Written Ministerial Statement (HCWS194), 7 July 2022 As above Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Clause 16(2) of Bill 121 2022-23 (PDF) would require Category 1 services to operate a service “using proportionate systems and processes designed to ensure that the importance of the free expression of journalistic content is taken into account when making decisions about”: (a) how to treat such content (especially decisions about whether to take it down or restrict users’ access to it), and (b) whether to take action against a user generating, uploading or sharing such content.

Clause 16(8) defines “journalistic content” as “news publisher content” or “regulated user-generated content” that is generated for the purposes of journalism and which is “UK-linked” (as defined in clause 16(9)). This would include content generated by news publishers, freelance journalists, and citizen journalists. There would be a duty, under clause 16(3), to make a dedicated and expedited complaints procedure available to a person when content was taken down, or access to it restricted, and the person considered it to be journalistic content. 378 Government new clause 19 was added to the Bill at report stage.379 Damian Collins, then Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the DCMS, explained that the new clause and associated amendments would require Category 1 services to notify a recognised news publisher and offer a right of appeal before removing or moderating its content or taking any action against its account. The Minister said this would reduce the risk of major platforms “taking over-zealous, arbitrary or accidental moderation decisions against news publisher content, which plays an invaluable role in UK democracy and society”. 380 He also explained that the new clause had been drafted so that platforms would not be required to provide an appeal before removing content that would give rise to civil or criminal liability to the service itself, or where it amounted to a relevant offence as defined by the Bill. 381 Damian Collins said the definition of “recognised news provider” would be tightened when the Bill was considered in the House of Lords so that sanctioned entities, such as RT, could not benefit from the Bill’s protections. 382 Amendments 167 and 168 were added to the Bill. 383 These would require Category 1 companies to assess the impact of their safety duties on how news publisher and journalistic content would be treated when hosted on a

chunks.text: a higher bar” for removing the protections in place for comments sections on recognised news publishers’ sites (see pp28-9 above) by ensuring that these could only be brought into the scope of regulation via primary legislation. 388 The DCMS published a fact sheet on the amendments relating to journalism. 389 Adult safety duties Government amendments 70 and 71 were added to the Bill. 390 Damian Collins said these clarified the policy intention of the clause 13 adult safety duties to improve transparency about how providers could treat harmful content, rather than incentivise its removal. 391 He said the changes were in response to stakeholder concerns that the Bill “did not make it sufficiently clear that providers could choose simply to allow any form of legal content, rather than promote, restrict or remove it, regardless of the harm to users”. The Minister added that this was “a really important point” that had sometimes been missed in discussion of the Bill. There was no requirement for companies to remove legal speech if their policies didn’t allow that: …There are very clear duties relating to illegal harm that companies must proactively identify and mitigate. The transparency requirements for other harmful content are very clear that companies must set out what their policies are. Enforcement action can be taken by the regulator for breach of their policies, but the primary objective is that companies make clear what their policies are. It is not a requirement for companies to remove legal speech if their policies do not allow that. 392

chunks.text: Online Safety Bill [online], 23 August 2022 (accessed 31 October 2023) 390 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c225 391 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c163 392 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c163 384 385 70 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Illegal content duties Assessing content Damian Collins said the purpose of Government new clause 14 was to clarify how in-scope services would determine whether they had duties regarding content on their services. 393 The effectiveness of companies’ safety measures would depend on them making reasonable judgments about types of content. It was therefore essential to have clarity about how providers should approach these judgments. According to the Minister, this would safeguard against companies over-removing innocuous content if they wrongly assumed mental elements were present, or under-removing content if they acted only where all elements of an offence had been established beyond reasonable doubt. The amendments would make clear that companies would have to consider “all reasonably available contextual information when determining whether content is illegal content, a fraudulent advert, content that is harmful to children, or content that is harmful to adults”. 394 For Labour, Alex Davies-Jones said new clause 14 was “deeply problematic” and was likely to significantly reduce the amount of illegal content and fraudulent advertising that was correctly identified and acted on. 395 Companies would be expected to determine whether content was illegal or fraudulently based on information that was “reasonably available to a provider”, with reasonableness determined in part by the size and capacity of the provider. Alex Davies-Jones claimed this could result in “malign actors” migrating illegal activity to smaller sites that had “less pronounced regulatory expectations placed on them”. At the other end of the scale, larger sites could use new clause 14 to argue that their size and capacity, and the large volume of material they were moderating, would make it impractical for them reliably and consistently to identify illegal content. 396 Under new clause 14, companies would need to have “reasonable grounds to infer that all elements necessary for the commission of the offence, including mental elements, are present or satisfied”. Alex Davies-Jones argued this would significantly raise the threshold at which companies would be likely to determine that content was illegal.397 She also claimed the new clause was not adequately future-proofed. 398 Alex Davies-Jones urged the Government to reconsider the new clause and related amendments and withdraw them.399

chunks.text: 12 July 2022 c168 396 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc168-9 397 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c169 398 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c169 399 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c169 393 394 71 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill New clause 14 was added to the Bill after a division (288 votes to 229). 400 Government new clause 15 would require Ofcom to give guidance to service providers about how they should approach judgements about whether content was illegal content or a fraudulent advertisement. New clause 15 was added to the Bill. 401 Service design Government amendments 58 to 61 were added to the Bill. 402 This was in response to concerns that the Bill did not adequately tackle activities such as “breadcrumbing” – ie where child sexual exploitation and abuse (CSEA) offenders post links or have conversations on a website that are preparatory to a CSEA offence that might occur on a different platform or offline. The Government explained: These amendments require providers to assess the risk of their services being used to commit or facilitate the commission of a priority offence, and to design their services to mitigate the risk of this occurring. This will ensure providers implement preventative measures to mitigate a broad spectrum of factors that enable illegal activity on their platforms, including where this might not result in ‘content’ on a platform which is clearly identifiable as being illegal. The amendments introduce an explicit duty on companies relating to the design of their services. Companies must take a safety-by-design approach, managing the risk of illegal content and activity appearing on their services, rather than focusing primarily on content moderation. In addition, these amendments make clear that platforms have duties to mitigate the risk of their service “facilitating” an offence, including where that offence may actually occur on another site - such as can occur in crossplatform CSEA offending… 403

chunks.text: illegal content should also be applied to any content which— (a) constitutes, (b) encourages, or (c) promotes violence against women or girls. (2) “Violence against women and girls” is defined by Article 3 of the Council of Europe Convention on Preventing Violence Against Women and Domestic Violence (“the Istanbul Convention”).”406 Alex Davies-Jones said the new clause was needed because, while Schedule 7 included a list of sexual offences and aggravated offences, the Government had not addressed the “wider context of violence against women and girls”. 407 Damian Collins argued that if clause 3 was approved, it would not be clear where the criminal threshold would apply: …New clause 3, perfectly properly, cites international conventions on violence against women and girls, and how that is defined. At the moment, with the way the Bill is structured, the schedule 7 offences are all based on existing areas of UK law, where there is an existing, clear criminal threshold. Those offences, which are listed extensively, will all apply as priority areas of harm. If there is, through the work of the Law Commission or elsewhere, a clear legal definition of misogyny and violence against women and girls that is not included, I think it should be included within scope. However, if new clause 3 was approved, as tabled, it would be a very different sort of offence, where it would not be as clear where the criminal threshold applied, because it is not cited against existing legislation… 408 The Minister claimed the “spirit of new clause 3 is reflected in the offences that are written into the Bill”. 409 New clause 3 was negatived by 292 votes to 226. 410

chunks.text: Deb 12 July 2022 c149 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c173 408 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc216-7 409 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c217 410 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc220-3 406 407 73 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Pornography New clause 7 and amendments 33 and 34, in the name of Dame Diana Johnson (Labour), would have required online platforms to verify the age and consent of all individuals featured in pornographic videos uploaded to their sites, as well as enabling individuals to withdraw their consent to the footage remaining on websites. 411 Damian Collins said the content addressed by the new clause was already illegal and there were legal duties on companies to act: 412 …The Bill will…creat[e] proactive responsibilities not just to discover illegal content but to act to mitigate it and to be audited to see how effectively it is done. Under the Bill, that now includes not just content that would be considered to be an abuse of children. A child cannot give consent to have sex or to appear in pornographic content. Companies need to make sure that what they are doing is sufficient to meet that need. It should be for the regulator, Ofcom, as part of putting together the codes of practice, to understand, even on more extreme content, what systems companies have in place to ensure that they are complying with the law and certainly not knowingly hosting content that has been flagged to them as being non-consensual pornography or child abuse images, which is effectively what pornography with minors would be… 413 New clause 7 was negatived by 285 votes to 220. 414

When speaking to the amendment, Barbara Keeley said that, without it, the Secretary of State would have the power to make decisions about priority content “quietly through secondary legislation”. She said any changes to priority content would be “worthy of proper debate”. 419 In response, Damian Collins said that when it came to adding a new priority offence to the Bill, the “premise” would be that it would already be an offence that Parliament had created and writing it into the Bill would “be done with the positive consent of Parliament”. 420 Amendment 31 was negatived on division by 283 votes to 188. 421 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc231-4 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c249 420 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c260 421 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc267-70 418 419 75 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 4 Government announcements on the Bill (November 2022)

chunks.text: 12 July 2022 cc231-4 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c249 420 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c260 421 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc267-70 418 419 75 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 4 Government announcements on the Bill (November 2022) On becoming Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport on 6 September 2022, Michelle Donelan reviewed the Bill’s adult safety duties (the “legal but harmful” provisions). According to the DCMS, this was to ensure the right balance was struck “between protecting users from harmful content online and protecting users’ rights to freedom of expression”. 422 On 28 November 2022, the DCMS announced the “legal but harmful” provisions would be replaced with new duties on free speech and the accountability of tech firms. The Bill’s proposed new harmful communications offence (clause 151) would also be removed to protect freedom of expression. Other changes to the Bill would include: • • • new measures to tackle violence against women and girls. strengthening protections for children. introducing an offence of sending a communication that encourages serious self-harm. 423
chunks.display_text: 12 July 2022 cc231-4 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c249 420 HC Deb 12 July 2022 c260 421 HC Deb 12 July 2022 cc267-70 418 419 75 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 4 Government announcements on the Bill (November 2022) On becoming Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport on 6 September 2022, Michelle Donelan reviewed the Bill’s adult safety duties (the “legal but harmful” provisions). According to the DCMS, this was to ensure the right balance was struck “between protecting users from harmful content online and protecting users’ rights to freedom of expression”. 422 On 28 November 2022, the DCMS announced the “legal but harmful” provisions would be replaced with new duties on free speech and the accountability of tech firms. The Bill’s proposed new harmful communications offence (clause 151) would also be removed to protect freedom of expression. Other changes to the Bill would include: • • • new measures to tackle violence against women and girls. strengthening protections for children. introducing an offence of sending a communication that encourages serious self-harm. 423

chunks.text: new harmful communications offence (clause 151) would also be removed to protect freedom of expression. Other changes to the Bill would include: • • • new measures to tackle violence against women and girls. strengthening protections for children. introducing an offence of sending a communication that encourages serious self-harm. 423 The Government also said it would introduce a new offence of “epilepsy trolling”, which involves sending or showing flashing images to an individual with epilepsy with the intention of causing them harm (such as a seizure). 424 A Written Ministerial Statement of 29 November 2022 was published on the changes. 425 This said that, to ensure there was proper scrutiny, some clauses would be returned to a Public Bill Committee. In a Written Statement of 30 November 2022, Paul Scully, then Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the DCMS, explained the process and said an “indicative drafting” of the amendments (PDF) had been published.426 On 25 November 2022, Dominic Raab, then Secretary of State for Justice, said the Government intended to legislate “to introduce a package of new criminal offences on intimate image abuse”. 427 This would include using the Bill to criminalise the sharing of people’s intimate images without their consent. chunks.text: Online Safety Bill: Update, Written Ministerial Statement (HCWS397), 29 November 2022 426 DCMS, Online Safety Bill: Government amendments Written Ministerial Statement (HCWS403), 30 November 2022 427 HC Deb 25 November 2022 c20WS 422 423 76 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Background to some of the Government’s proposed changes is set out below. 4.1 Legal but harmful content The Bill’s provisions on Category 1 services and content that may be “legal but harmful” for adults have been controversial. One of the concerns raised by some commentators and organisations has been that platforms could “overremove” legal content, affecting freedom of expression. However, the Carnegie UK Trust and civil society campaigners have said the Bill’s provisions in this area are needed to protect people whose right to free speech is threatened by hate speech or online abuse which is intended to intimidate or silence them.428 For further discussion and comment see: • section 7.1 of the Library Briefing, Analysis of the Online Safety Bill (PDF) (8 April 2022). • Library Briefing, The Online Safety Bill: A reading list (PDF) (2 May 2023). In a DCMS press release of 28 November 2022, the Secretary of State announced that the Bill’s “legal but harmful” provisions would be replaced with new duties to “boost free speech and increase accountability of tech firms”. 429 The changes would offer adult users a “triple shield” of protection ie platforms would be legally required to: • remove illegal content. • take down material in breach of their terms of service. • provide adults with greater choice over the content they see and engage with.

One of the concerns raised by some commentators and organisations has been that platforms could “overremove” legal content, affecting freedom of expression. However, the Carnegie UK Trust and civil society campaigners have said the Bill’s provisions in this area are needed to protect people whose right to free speech is threatened by hate speech or online abuse which is intended to intimidate or silence them.428 For further discussion and comment see: • section 7.1 of the Library Briefing, Analysis of the Online Safety Bill (PDF) (8 April 2022). • Library Briefing, The Online Safety Bill: A reading list (PDF) (2 May 2023). In a DCMS press release of 28 November 2022, the Secretary of State announced that the Bill’s “legal but harmful” provisions would be replaced with new duties to “boost free speech and increase accountability of tech firms”. 429 The changes would offer adult users a “triple shield” of protection ie platforms would be legally required to: • remove illegal content. • take down material in breach of their terms of service. • provide adults with greater choice over the content they see and engage with.

chunks.text: tech firms”. 429 The changes would offer adult users a “triple shield” of protection ie platforms would be legally required to: • remove illegal content. • take down material in breach of their terms of service. • provide adults with greater choice over the content they see and engage with. The press release claimed the changes would strike the right balance between protecting adults and protecting free speech: The Bill will instead give adults greater control over online posts they may not wish to see on platforms. If users are likely to encounter certain types of content - such as the glorification of eating disorders, racism, anti-semitism or misogyny not meeting the criminal threshold - internet companies will have to offer adults tools to help them avoid it. These could include human moderation, blocking content flagged by other users or sensitivity and warning screens.
