In addition, the Victims’ Commissioner and the Domestic Abuse Commissioner would be named as statutory consultees to ensure they would be consulted by Ofcom ahead of drafting and amending codes of practice. 436 4.4 Harmful communications The Government tabled amendments for report stage to remove the proposed new harmful communications offence set out in clause 151 of the Bill (as amended in Committee). 437 The related proposed offences of false and threatening communications (clauses 152 and 153 of the Bill as amended in Committee) would remain in the Bill. The harmful communications offence would have been committed if a person sends a message and at the time of sending it: • there was a “real and substantial risk that it would cause harm to a likely audience”, with harm defined as “psychological harm amounting to at least serious distress” • the person intended to cause harm to a likely audience • the person had no reasonable excuse for sending the message The Government said it is removing the proposed offence from the Bill because of concerns about freedom of expression and proportionality: Stakeholders and members of the public have expressed concerns this offence has the potential to produce unintended consequences on freedom of expression as they believe it would criminalise legal and legitimate speech on the basis that it has caused someone offence. The Digital Secretary has decided to remove the harmful communications offence from the Bill to ensure the Bill’s measures are proportionate and do not unduly criminalise content that some may find offensive. Platforms can, and in most cases do, already have terms of service that relate to such content and so would be captured as part of the ‘triple shield’. 438

New protections for children and free speech added to internet laws, DCMS press release [online] 28 November 2022 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 4.5 Encouraging serious self-harm In her Written Ministerial Statement of 29 November 2022, Michelle Donelan said the Government would be bringing forward an amendment in the Lords to introduce an offence of sending a communication that encourages serious self-harm: I am aware of particular concerns around content online which encourages vulnerable people to self-harm. While the child safety duties in the Bill will protect children, vulnerable adults may remain at risk of exposure to this abhorrent content. I am therefore committing to making the encouragement of self-harm illegal. The government will bring forward in this Bill proposals to create an offence of sending a communication that encourages serious selfharm via an amendment in the House of Lords. This new offence will ensure that trolls sending such messages to a person, regardless of the recipient’s age, face the consequences for their vile actions. 439 The Molly Rose Foundation, a suicide prevention charity established by the friends and family of Molly Russell, said the proposal “appears a significant move”. However, the Foundation said it was important the Bill also covered other types of “harmful but legal” content. 440 4.6

However, the Foundation said it was important the Bill also covered other types of “harmful but legal” content. 440 4.6 Intimate image abuse Intimate image abuse involves the non-consensual taking, making and/or sharing of intimate images. There is currently no single criminal offence that covers intimate image abuse. Instead, a range of offences exist covering different types of image and different types of conduct. 441 In a recent review of the law on intimate images the Law Commission described the existing criminal law as a “patchwork” and noted the offences had failed to keep up with developments in technology and sexual offending. 442 The Law Commission therefore recommended that the Government replace the existing offences with a new tiered framework, including a ‘base’ offence of taking or sharing an intimate image without consent without the need to prove any additional motivation/intent on the part of the offender. Government new clause 11 was added to the Bill to replace clause 104. 446 Paul Scully explained it would empower Ofcom to require companies to: • use accredited technology to identify and remove illegal terrorism and CSEA content or to prevent users from encountering that content; or • use their “best endeavours” to develop or to source technology to tackle CSEA. 447

Government new clause 11 was added to the Bill to replace clause 104. 446 Paul Scully explained it would empower Ofcom to require companies to: • use accredited technology to identify and remove illegal terrorism and CSEA content or to prevent users from encountering that content; or • use their “best endeavours” to develop or to source technology to tackle CSEA. 447

chunks.text: 104. 446 Paul Scully explained it would empower Ofcom to require companies to: • use accredited technology to identify and remove illegal terrorism and CSEA content or to prevent users from encountering that content; or • use their “best endeavours” to develop or to source technology to tackle CSEA. 447 The new clause would make clear that technological tools could seek to proactively prevent CSEA content from appearing on a service, rather than focusing only on identification and removal after it had been found. Paul Scully said this would ensure the best possible protection for children, including on services that offered livestreaming. 448 He also claimed the changes would strike the “right balance of supporting the adoption of new technology, while ensuring that it does not come at the expense of children’s physical safety”. 449 Government new clause 12 was added to the Bill. 450 This would also replace part of clause 104 and would make additions to the warning notice procedure to take account of the new options for notices under new clause 11. chunks.text: several related amendments) to add a new offence of ‘epilepsy trolling’ to the Bill. 451 New clause 53 would make it an offence for a person to send an electronic communication consisting of or including flashing images, without reasonable excuse, where either condition 1 or condition 2 is met: • condition 1 is that at the time the communication is sent, it was reasonably foreseeable that an individual with epilepsy would be among the individuals who would view it and the sender intended that such an individual would suffer harm as a result of viewing the flashing images. • condition 2 is that when sending the communication, the sender believed that an individual whom the sender knew or suspected to be an individual with epilepsy would, or might, view it and the sender intended that individual would suffer harm as a result of viewing the flashing images. New clause 53 would also make it an offence for a person to show an individual flashing images by means of an electronic communications device, where the person knows or suspects that individual has epilepsy and intends they will suffer harm as a result of viewing the images. New clause 53 would apply to England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Introducing new clause 53, Paul Scully said it would capture communications shared “in a scatter-gun manner—for example, by sharing a flashing image on social media” as well as “the more targeted sending of flashing images to a person who the sender knows or suspects is a person with epilepsy”. 452 He said under new clause 53 “those seeking to cause harm to people with epilepsy will face appropriate criminal sanctions”. 453 New clause 53 received cross-party support and was added to the Bill without a vote.

For the Opposition, Alex Davies-Jones described the Government’s decision as “an incredible overturn” that would have “real-life consequences”: It will mean that harmful online trends such as hoax bomb threats, abusive social media pile-ons and fake news such as encouraging people to drink bleach to cure covid will be allowed to spread online without any consequence. 457 The amendments were agreed without a vote and the harmful communications offence has been removed from the Bill. Encouraging or assisting self-harm New clause 16, in the name of David Davis, would have amended the Suicide Act 1961 to create a new communication offence for sending a message encouraging or assisting another person to self-harm. 464 For the Government, Paul Scully said he was “absolutely aligned” with the objectives of the new clause. However, amending the 1961 Act was not the right place to introduce measures on self-harm. The Government would work to ensure it got the provisions “absolutely right” as the Bill proceeded to the House of Lords. 465 Mr Davis acknowledged the difficulties in drafting the provisions and how they would interact with other legislation (eg the 1961 Act). However, he wanted the new clause added to the Bill, even it was “not quite perfect”, as it could be amended in the Lords. He would therefore press the new clause to a vote. 466 New clause 16 was negatived by 308 votes to 242. 467 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c49 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c123 463 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c50 464 HC Deb 5 December 2022 cc24-5 465 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c48 466 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c79 467 HC Deb 5 December 2022 cc129-32 461 462 85 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: 5 December 2022 c50 464 HC Deb 5 December 2022 cc24-5 465 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c48 466 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c79 467 HC Deb 5 December 2022 cc129-32 461 462 85 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Director liability New clause 17, in the name of Dame Margaret Hodge, would have enabled Ofcom to use its enforcement powers against individual directors, managers, and other officers at a regulated service provider. 468 Dame Margaret Hodge said that if Members really wanted to reduce the amount of harmful abuse online, then making senior directors personally liable was “a very good way of achieving it”. She said that 82% of UK adults agreed, as did the Labour Front Bench and Back Benchers across the House. 469 Alex Davies-Jones said the new clause would “fix the frustrating gaps in Ofcom’s enforcement powers” by making senior executives “take notice and make positive and meaningful changes”. 470 For the Government, Paul Scully said that expanding the scope of individual liability under the Bill could damage the UK’s attractiveness as a place to start a digital business. It could also have unintended consequences, such as tech executives taking an “over-zealous approach to content take-down, for fear of going to prison for a regulatory failing”. 471 Paul Scully said he was confident that Ofcom would have “all the tools necessary” to drive culture change in the sector from the boardroom down. 472 New clause 16 was negatived by 311 votes to 238. 473

However, he pointed out that Ofcom would have “comprehensive powers to force tech companies to keep children safe online”. Only the recommitted clauses and schedules, plus amendments and new clauses relating to them, were in scope for consideration. There were two Committee sittings on 13 December 2022 and one sitting on 15 December 2022. Government amendments and new clauses were agreed. No other amendments or new clauses were agreed. A summary of some of the areas covered in the sittings is set out below. 6.1
chunks.display_text: Deb 5 December 2022 cc170-2 480 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c165 481 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c164 482 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c166 483 HC Deb 5 December 2022 c168 484 HC Deb 5 December 2022 cc163-72 476 477 87 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 6 Additional Commons Public Bill Committee debates (December 2022) As noted earlier in this Briefing (see p75), on 29 November 2022 the Government announced that some clauses of the Bill would be returned to Public Bill Committee so that the Government’s proposed changes could be properly scrutinised. 485 The recommitted clauses and schedules were: clauses 11 to 14, 18 to 21, 30, 46, 55 and 65, schedule 8, clauses 79 and 82, schedule 11, clauses 87, 90, 115, 169 and 183, schedule 17, clauses 203, 206 and 207. chunks.text: scope for consideration. There were two Committee sittings on 13 December 2022 and one sitting on 15 December 2022. Government amendments and new clauses were agreed. No other amendments or new clauses were agreed. A summary of some of the areas covered in the sittings is set out below. 6.1 Public Bill Committee (13 December 2022) Protecting children Age assurance Government amendments 1, 2 and 3 to clause 11 (safety duties protecting children) were agreed. 486 Paul Scully explained that the Bill already set out that age assurance could be required to protect children from harmful content and activity, as part of meeting the duty in clause 11(3). The amendments would mean that age assurance might also be needed to meet the wider duty in subsection (2) - ie to “mitigate and manage the risks of harm to children” and to manage “the impact of harm to children”. Paul Scully said this was important so that only children who were old enough would be able to use functionalities on a service that posed a risk of harm to younger children. The changes would also ensure that children were signposted to support that was appropriate to their age if they experienced harm. 487 485 486 487 88 DCMS, Online Safety Bill: Update, Written Ministerial Statement (HCWS397), 29 November 2022 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c13 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c13 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: if they experienced harm. 487 485 486 487 88 DCMS, Online Safety Bill: Update, Written Ministerial Statement (HCWS397), 29 November 2022 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c13 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c13 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Terms of service Government amendments 4 and 5 were agreed. 488 These would require service providers who already had a minimum age requirement for access to their service (or parts of it) to give details in their terms of services of the measures they used to ensure that children below the minimum age were prevented access. Those terms would have to be applied consistently and be clear and accessible to users. Paul Scully said the change would mean that providers could be held to account for what was said in their terms of service and would no longer do nothing to prevent underage access. 489 Protecting adults Removing the provisions on “legal but harmful” content On 28 November 2022, the Government announced its intention to remove the “legal but harmful” provisions from the Bill. 490 On 13 December 2022, the Committee debated the removal of the relevant clauses: • • • clause 12 (adults’ risk assessment duties). clause 13 (safety duties protecting adults). clause 55 (content that is harmful to adults etc).

Those terms would have to be applied consistently and be clear and accessible to users. Paul Scully said the change would mean that providers could be held to account for what was said in their terms of service and would no longer do nothing to prevent underage access. 489 Protecting adults Removing the provisions on “legal but harmful” content On 28 November 2022, the Government announced its intention to remove the “legal but harmful” provisions from the Bill. 490 On 13 December 2022, the Committee debated the removal of the relevant clauses: • • • clause 12 (adults’ risk assessment duties). clause 13 (safety duties protecting adults). clause 55 (content that is harmful to adults etc).

chunks.text: its intention to remove the “legal but harmful” provisions from the Bill. 490 On 13 December 2022, the Committee debated the removal of the relevant clauses: • • • clause 12 (adults’ risk assessment duties). clause 13 (safety duties protecting adults). clause 55 (content that is harmful to adults etc). For the Government, Paul Scully explained that the clauses were being removed to protect free speech and remove any possibility that the Bill could cause companies to censor legal content. 491 However, he also recognised the “undue influence” that the largest platforms could have over public discourse: …These companies get to decide what we do and do not see online. They can arbitrarily remove a user’s content or ban them altogether without offering any real avenues of redress to users. On the flip side, even when companies have terms of service, these are often not enforced, as we have discussed. That was the case after the Euro 2020 final where footballers were subject to the most appalling abuse, despite most platforms clearly prohibiting that. 492 For this reason, the Government would be introducing duties to improve the transparency and accountability of platforms and to protect free speech through new clauses 3 and 4: …Under these duties, category 1 platforms will only be allowed to remove or restrict access to content or ban or suspend users when this is in accordance with their terms of service or where they face another legal obligation. That protects against the arbitrary removal of content. chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c24 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c20 490 New protections for children and free speech added to internet laws, DCMS press release [online], 28 November 2022 491 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c24 492 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c25 488 489 89 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Companies must ensure that their terms of service are consistently enforced. If companies’ terms of service say that they will remove or restrict access to content, or will ban or suspend users in certain circumstances, they must put in place proper systems and processes to apply those terms. That will close the gap between what companies say they will do and what they do in practice. Services must ensure that their terms of service are easily understandable to users and that they operate effective reporting and redress mechanisms, enabling users to raise concerns about a company’s application of the terms of service… 493 Alex Davies-Jones said the Government’s approach was “very wrong” and that Labour couldn’t support the amendments: Ultimately, platforms should be required to protect everyone: it does not matter whether they are a 17-year-old who falls short of being legally deemed an adult in this country, an 18-year-old or even an 80-year-old. 494

chunks.text: said the Government’s approach was “very wrong” and that Labour couldn’t support the amendments: Ultimately, platforms should be required to protect everyone: it does not matter whether they are a 17-year-old who falls short of being legally deemed an adult in this country, an 18-year-old or even an 80-year-old. 494 She also claimed that removing the “legal but harmful” provisions was “throwing the baby out with the bathwater” and urged the Government to keep them in the Bill: …It was a phrase we all disliked, and it did not encapsulate exactly what the content is or includes…this is a tricky area, and it is difficult to get it right. We need to protect free speech, which is sacrosanct, but we also need to recognise that there are so many users on the internet who do not have access to free speech as a result of being piled on or shouted down. Their free speech needs to be protected too. We believe that the clauses as they stand in the Bill go some way to making the Bill a meaningful piece of legislation. I urge the Minister not to strip them out, to do the right thing and to keep them in the Bill. 495

chunks.text: needs to be protected too. We believe that the clauses as they stand in the Bill go some way to making the Bill a meaningful piece of legislation. I urge the Minister not to strip them out, to do the right thing and to keep them in the Bill. 495 Damian Collins, the former DCMS Minister and Chair of the Joint Committee on the draft Bill argued that removing the clauses would not result in a “wild west” and that the Bill would enable “very effective enforcement”: I personally believed that the “legal but harmful” provisions in the Bill, as far as they existed, were not an infringement on free speech, because there was never a requirement to remove legal speech. I do not think the removal of those clauses from the Bill suddenly creates a wild west in which no enforcement will take place at all. There will be very effective enforcement based on the terms of service, and on the schedule 7 offences, which deal with the worst kinds of illegal activity; there is a broad list. The changes make it much clearer to everybody—platforms and users alike, and Ofcom—exactly what the duties are, how they are enforced and what they are based on. 496 Kirsty Blackman criticised the removal of the clauses and claimed the Bill would make people less safe online: …We will continue to have the same problems that we have with people being driven to suicide and radicalised online as a result of the changes being made Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c25 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c27 495 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c29 496 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c34 493 494 90 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023

I do not think the removal of those clauses from the Bill suddenly creates a wild west in which no enforcement will take place at all. There will be very effective enforcement based on the terms of service, and on the schedule 7 offences, which deal with the worst kinds of illegal activity; there is a broad list. The changes make it much clearer to everybody—platforms and users alike, and Ofcom—exactly what the duties are, how they are enforced and what they are based on. 496 Kirsty Blackman criticised the removal of the clauses and claimed the Bill would make people less safe online: …We will continue to have the same problems that we have with people being driven to suicide and radicalised online as a result of the changes being made Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c25 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c27 495 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c29 496 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c34 493 494 90 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023

chunks.text: does not adhere to the companies’ terms of service—that includes many of the issues that we have been debating for the last hour—it will have to be removed. 498 Clauses 12 and 13 were removed from the Bill after divisions (9 votes to 6). 499 Clause 55 was also removed.500 User empowerment duties The Government intends the Bill’s user empowerment duties to form part of a “triple shield” of protection for adults. 501 Under clause 14 of the Bill, Category 1 services would need to have tools in place to allow adult users to control what harmful content they see. Government amendments 9 and 12 would mean companies would have to ensure that these tools were effective in reducing the likelihood of encountering listed content or alerting users to it. The tools would also have to be easy to access. Paul Scully said the changes would give adult users greater control over their online experience. 502 Government amendment 15 would set out the categories of content that the user empowerment tools would apply to: • content encouraging, promoting or providing instructions for: – – • suicide or an act of deliberate self-injury. an eating disorder or behaviours associated with an eating disorder. abusive content that targeted any of the following characteristics: – – – – – race. religion. sex. sexual orientation. disability. Paul Scully said the Government had taken a “targeted approach, focused on areas where we know that adult users - particularly those who are vulnerable or disproportionately targeted by online hate and abuse - would benefit from having greater choice.” 504 He emphasised that the user empowerment duties would not require companies to remove legal content from their services. The duties were about giving individual adult users the option to increase their control over certain categories of content. 505 Alex Davies-Jones said Labour had concerns about the overall approach of the Government but believed that “important additions” could be made to the list of content where user-empowerment tools would have to be in place.506 Labour’s amendment (a) to Government amendment 15 would add the following: • • • content that incited hateful extremism. content that provided false information about climate change. content that was harmful to health. 507

She also observed that “malicious actors” spreading false information on social media could undermine collective action to combat extremism and climate change. 509 Alex Davies-Jones said Labour’s amendments sought to ensure that the omission from the Bill of hateful extremism was “put right here as a priority”. 510 Kim Leadbeater spoke at length about extremism and criticised the removal of the “legal but harmful” clauses: …I am particularly upset that a few Conservative Members appear to believe that such content should remain unchecked online because of free speech, with full knowledge that it is exactly that content that serves as the gateway for people to self-harm and to be radicalised. That is not free speech. 511

That is not free speech. 511 Kirsty Blackman said the SNP “wholeheartedly” agreed with Labour’s amendments. 512 Paul Scully resisted Labour’s amendments. He said, among other things, that including harmful health content as a category risked requiring companies to apply the adult user empowerment tools to “an unfeasibly large volume of content—way beyond just the vaccine efficacy that was mentioned”. This could have implications both for regulatory burden and for freedom of expression, as it could capture important health advice.513 He also said the Bill would “likely capture” most of the content that the Opposition parties were seeking to capture through the hateful extremism category – eg antisemitic abuse and conspiracy theories, racist abuse and the promotion of racist ideologies. 514 Labour’s amendment (a) to Government amendment 15 was negatived by 8 votes to 6. 515 The Government’s amendments 15 and 16 were made. 516 Labour’s amendments 102 and 103 to clause 14 would have required platforms to have their user empowerment tools “on” by default. 517 When 508 509 510 511 512 513 514 515 516 517 93 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c49 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 cc52-3 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c53 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c58 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c54 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c59 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c60 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 c68 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 cc68-9 Public Bill Committee 13 December 2022 cc61-2 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023

Ofcom would oversee companies’ systems and processes for discharging those duties. 521 Government new clause 4 would require Category 1 services to ensure that their content moderation policies, as set out in their terms of service, were clear and accessible so that users would know what to expect in relation to moderation actions. Systems and processes would have to be in place to enable the terms of service to be enforced consistently. 522 The duties would give users the ability to report any content or account if they thought it failed to meet a platform’s terms of service. Users would also be able to make complaints about platforms’ moderation actions and raise concerns if their content was removed in error. Providers would have to take appropriate action in response to complaints – eg removing content they prohibited, or reinstating content removed in error. 523 Government new clause 5 would require Ofcom to publish guidance to help Category 1 services understand what they would need to do to comply with the duties.524 Paul Scully said the new clauses would “fundamentally reset the relationship between platforms and their users”: …The duties will prevent services from arbitrarily removing content or suspending users without offering users proper avenues to appeal. That would likely weaken the duties’ impact on tackling criminal activity and protecting children. 538 Amendment 104 was negatived by 8 votes to 4. 539 532 533 534 535 536 537 538 539 96 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c104 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c104 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c106 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c109 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c109 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c111 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c112 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c113 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c109 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c109 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c111 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c112 Public Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c113 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Ofcom guidance Government new clause 1 was added to the Bill. 540 This would require Ofcom to give guidance to providers in relation to the kinds of content that it considered to be harmful content to children and content relevant to the duty in clause 14(2) (user empowerment). Paul Scully said it was “really important” that expert opinion was considered when developing the guidance. The new clause would therefore place a duty on Ofcom to consult with relevant persons when producing sets of guidance. 541 Alex Davies-Jones welcomed the new clause, although she said Labour remained concerned about how the Bill’s user empowerment tools would work in practice. 542 Kirsty Blackman said the SNP also supported the new clause. 543 Child user empowerment duties Kirsty Blackman moved new clause 8. 544 This would have introduced child user duties in relation to Category 1 services. The duties would have included: • • • allowing children to have a “white list” of people they wanted to be contacted by. requiring a service to include features that children could apply to filter out private messages from non-verified users, adult users, or any user other than those on the white list. requiring a service to include, where it was proportionate to do so, features that children could apply if they wanted to increase their control over harmful content.

chunks.text: Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Paul Scully resisted new clause 8. He said the Bill’s safety duties to protect children from harm meant they would not need the tools that the clause made provision for. 547 New clause 8 was negatived by 8 votes to 4. 548 Senior management liability New clause 9, in the name of Sir William Cash, was moved by Nick Fletcher. 549 This dealt with senior manager liability. When speaking to the new clause, Nick Fletcher explained that senior managers would not be personally liable for breaching the Bill’s safety duties. They would only be liable for failing to comply with information requests or for willingly seeking to mislead Ofcom. Nick Fletcher said the Bill needed strengthening to “actively promote cultural change in companies and embed compliance with online safety regulations at board level”: …We need a robust corporate and senior management liability scheme that imposes personal liability on directors whose actions consistently and significantly put children at risk. The Bill must learn lessons from other regulated sectors, principally financial services, where regulation imposes specific duties on the directors and senior managers of financial institutions, and those responsible individuals face regulatory enforcement if they act in breach of such duties. 550

The Bill must learn lessons from other regulated sectors, principally financial services, where regulation imposes specific duties on the directors and senior managers of financial institutions, and those responsible individuals face regulatory enforcement if they act in breach of such duties. 550 Alex Davies-Jones said Labour supported the new clause and argued that the Bill had to put online safety at the forefront of business models. A robust senior management liability scheme was required, and it needed to impose personal liability on directors when they put children at risk. 551 Paul Scully claimed that innovation would be stifled if people were threatened with personal legal liability. In addition, the threat of criminal prosecution for failing to comply with various duties could damage the attractiveness of the UK as a place to start a digital business. 552 Nick Fletcher did not put new clause 9 to a division but said the issue of liability would be returned to when the Bill had its remaining stages. 553 The Bill [Bill 220 2022/23] (PDF), as amended after its recommittal to Committee, was published. chunks.text: Bill Committee 15 December 2022 c145 547 548 98 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 7 Commons remaining stages (17 January 2023) The Bill’s remaining stages (ie report stage day three and third reading) took place on 17 January 2023. 7.1 Written Ministerial Statement (17 January 2023) On 17 January 2023, the Government published a Written Ministerial Statement explaining that it would be amending the Bill in the following areas after “constructive discussions” with Members. 554 Senior management liability In response to new clause 2, in the name of Sir William Cash, the Government said it would table an amendment in the Lords. This would be based on Ireland’s Online Safety and Media Regulation Act 2022 (PDF). 555 Illegal immigration In response to amendment 82, in the name of Mrs Natalie Elphick, the Government said it would add section 2 of the Modern Slavery Act 2015 to the Bill’s list of priority offences. Section 2 of the 2015 Act makes it an offence to arrange or facilitate the travel of another person, including through recruitment, with a view to their exploitation. Section 24 of the Immigration Act 1971 would also be added to the priority offences set out in schedule 7. The Written Statement explained that although the offences in section 24 cannot be carried out online, paragraph 33 of schedule 7 states that priority illegal content includes the inchoate offences relating to the offences that are listed: …Therefore, aiding, abetting, counselling, conspiring etc. those offences by posting videos of people crossing the channel that show that activity in a positive light could be an offence that is committed online and therefore falls within what is priority illegal content. 556 554 555 556 99

chunks.text: 557 Conversion therapy In response to Alicia Kearns’ amendment 84, the Government said it would publish a draft Bill on banning conversion practices. The legislation would apply to England and Wales and would “protect everyone, including those targeted on the basis of their sexuality or for being transgender”. 558 7.2 Report stage: day three During the debate, Paul Scully confirmed that the Government would be strengthening the Bill’s provisions on senior management liability at the end of “ping-pong” with the House of Lords. The amendments would be based on the Irish Online Safety and Media Regulation Act 2022. This has introduced individual criminal liability for failure to comply with the notice to end contravention: …In line with that approach, the final Government amendment, at the end of the ping-pong between the other place and this place, will be carefully designed to capture instances in which senior managers, or those purporting to act in that capacity, have consented or connived in ignoring enforceable requirements, risking serious harm to children. The criminal penalties, including imprisonment or fines, will be commensurate with those applying to similar offences. While the amendment will not affect those who have acted in good faith to comply in a proportionate way, it will give the Act additional teeth…to deliver the change that we all want, and ensure that people are held to account if they fail to protect children properly. 559

chunks.text: c277 562 HC Deb 17 January 2023 cc314-5 557 558 100 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Government amendments were made to the Bill. These were consequential on removing the “legal but harmful” provisions when the Bill was recommitted to Committee. Divisions on Opposition amendments New clause 4 would have enabled Ofcom to set minimum standards in relation to the safety duties protecting adults and society. Alex Davies-Jones said the new clause was necessary to ensure that platforms could not avoid their safety duties by changing their terms and conditions whenever they saw fit. She pointed out that Elon Musk had lifted Twitter’s ban on Covid disinformation following his takeover of the company. 563 She also referred to a “grey area of incredibly harmful but legal content” that was proliferating online, being widely shared, and causing harm and disinformation. 564 Paul Scully said it was not appropriate for the Government to decide whether legal content was harmful to adult users and then require companies to risk assess and set terms for such content. 565 New clause 4 was negatived on division by 310 votes to 242. 566 Amendment 43 to clause 12 would have required the adult safety toggle feature to be turned on by default. When speaking to her amendment, Kirsty Blackman said that adults should have to opt in to receive material such as eating disorder content, or racist or “incredibly harmful content that does not meet the bar of illegality”: … We should start from the position of having the safest option on. We are trying to improve the permissive approach that the Government have arrived at, and this simple change is not controversial. It would require users to flip a switch if they want to opt in to some of the worst and most dangerous content available online, including pro-suicide, pro-anorexia or pro-bulimia content, rather than leaving that switch on by default… 567

chunks.text: “much more effective”.571 However, Lucy Powell said the Government had “watered down the Bill significantly”. 572 571 572 102 HC Deb 17 January 2023 cc329-30 HC Deb 17 January 2023 c332 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill 8 Lords second reading The Bill, as amended, was introduced in the House of Lords on 18 January 2023. 573 Explanatory Notes to the Bill were also published. 574 Second reading took place on 1 February 2023. 575 Lord Parkinson of Whitley Bay, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Arts and Heritage and DCMS Lords Minister, confirmed that the Government would be tabling amendments to: • name the Children’s Commissioner for England as a statutory consultee for Ofcom when it was preparing a code of practice. • specify that Category 1 companies would be required to publish a summary of their risk assessments for both illegal content and material that was harmful to children. • widen the personal liability of senior managers where their actions risked serious harm to children. • list controlling or coercive behaviour as a priority offence. • name the Victims’ Commissioner and the Domestic Abuse Commissioner as statutory consultees for codes of practice. • change the Secretary of State’s power regarding direction in relation to codes of practice – ie 1) replacing the “public policy” wording with a defined list of reasons that a direction could be made; and 2) making it clear that this element of the power could only be used in exceptional circumstances. • add an offence to address the encouragement or assistance of selfharm communications and offences addressing intimate image abuse online, including deep-fake pornography. 576

chunks.text: in Committee between 25 April and 22 June 2023 where Government amendments were agreed. No Opposition amendments were successful. The Bill was considered on report on 6, 10, 12, 17 and 19 July 2023. Government amendments were again made to the Bill. The Government was defeated on three issues: • adding animal welfare issues to schedule 7 (Priority offences). This became clause 64 of the Bill as amended on report (HL Bill 164 (PDF)). • an amendment to clause 11 (Children’s risk assessment duties) of HL Bill 164. The amendment would mean that in carrying out risk assessments, user-to-user services would have to “consider the potential for the design and operation of services to create harm separately and additionally to harm relating to the dissemination of or encountering harmful content”. • an amendment to schedule 11 (Categories of regulated user-to-user services and regulated search services: regulations). This would mean that the test for determining which services would fall within Category 1 would move from a test of size “and” functionality to a test of size “or” functionality. The Bill had its third reading on 6 September 2023. This section of the Briefing gives an overview of some of the areas on which Government amendments were made. It also gives further detail on the nonGovernment amendments. It is not intended to be a comprehensive discussion. Amendments which are minor, technical, or consequential are also not examined. The clause would make clear that regulated services would have to identify, mitigate, and manage risks that particularly affected people with certain characteristics. The Minister said this recognised that people with certain characteristics were “disproportionately affected by online harms and that providers must account for and protect them from this”. 584 The new clause would also make clear that a main outcome of the Bill would be that services would have to be safe by design. 585 9.2

chunks.text: that people with certain characteristics were “disproportionately affected by online harms and that providers must account for and protect them from this”. 584 The new clause would also make clear that a main outcome of the Bill would be that services would have to be safe by design. 585 9.2 Risk assessments When clause 6 (providers of user-to-user services: duties of care) 586 was debated in Committee, Lord Parkinson moved Government amendments relating to risk assessments. 587 He explained that Ofcom and services’ risk assessments would form the foundation for protecting users from illegal content and content that was harmful to children. They would ensure that providers identified the risks on their websites, enabling them to manage and mitigate the potential harms. Ofcom would set out the risks across the sector and issue guidance on how companies could conduct their assessments effectively. All providers would have to carry out risk assessments, keep them up-to-date and update them before making a significant change to the design or operation of their service which could put their users at risk. Providers would then need to put in place measures to manage and mitigate the risks identified in their risk assessments, including any emerging risks. 588 The Minister explained that the amendments would place new duties on providers of the largest services (ie Category 1 and 2A services) to: 583 584 585 586 587 588 106 • publish summaries of their illegal and child safety risk assessments. • send full records of their risk assessments to Ofcom. • make summaries of the assessments publicly available – to empower users to make informed decisions when choosing whether and how to use services.

Lord Parkinson explained that the amendments were intended to rebalance the “power over the content adults see and interact with online, moving the choice away from unaccountable technology companies and towards individual users”. 598 Clause 12 was amended to impose a new duty on providers of Category 1 services to proactively ask all registered adult users whether they wished to opt in or opt out of any features offered in compliance with the duty in subsection (2), until a choice was made. 599 The Government also added a new clause to the Bill 600 requiring Category 1 services to carry out and update as necessary an assessment about how likely would be that adult users would encounter suicide and self-harm content. 601

chunks.text: to children. 602 Primary priority content would include: • pornographic content. • content that encouraged, promoted, or provided instructions for suicide, self-harm, or eating disorders. Designating the above as primary priority content would mean the most stringent child safety duties would apply.603 Priority content harmful to children would include: • content that depicts real or realistic serious violence or injury. This includes depictions of physical fighting where there is serious physical harm to the individuals depicted, serious violence to an animal, and graphic depictions of a serious injury such as an open wound. • content that encourages, promotes or provides instructions for an act of serious violence. For example, the glorification or glamorisation of violence. • content which encourages, promotes or provides instructions for a challenge or stunt highly likely to result in serious injury. For example, content encouraging asphyxiation challenges. • content that encourages the ingestion, inhalation or exposure to harmful substances. For example, the promotion of dangerous DIY abortion methods. • content that is abusive or incites hate on the basis of race, religion, disability, sex, sexual orientation, or gender reassignment. This will include legal forms of racist, homophobic, and sexist abuse. • bullying, including threats, and degrading or humiliating content.604 HL Deb 6 July 2023 c1384 HL Deb 6 July 2023 c1384. This would ensure that platforms would not be required to offer publishers a right of appeal every time they proposed carrying out routine content curation as this would be “unworkable” and “likely to inhibit the effectiveness of the appeal process”. 608 Clause 52 (restricting users’ access to content) 609 was also amended to ensure that providers would only be required to offer publishers a right of appeal in relation to their own moderation decisions, not where a user had voluntarily chosen not to view certain types of content. Lord Parkinson explained: …our amendments clarify that any video or audio content published or broadcast by recognised news publishers will be exempt from the Bill’s safety duties and will benefit from the news publisher appeals process, when shared on platforms in scope of the Bill. These amendments ensure that old terminology works effectively in the internet age. The amendments now also make it clear that any news publisher content that is clipped or edited by the publisher itself will qualify for the Bill’s protections when shared by third parties on social media. However, these protections will not apply when a third-party user modifies that content itself. This will ensure that the protections do not apply to news publisher content that has been edited by a user in a potentially harmful way. 612

chunks.text: 618 Ofcom would be required to consult the Domestic Abuse Commissioner and the Victims’ Commissioner, among other people or organisations it considered appropriate, when creating the guidance. This would ensure that the voices of experts, women, girls, and victims, were reflected. Ofcom would be required to publish the guidance. 619 Powers of the Secretary of State Government amendments to clause 38 (Procedure for issuing codes of practice) 620 were agreed at report stage. The clause sets out the Secretary of State’s powers to direct Ofcom, issue guidance to Ofcom and set strategic priorities. When speaking to the amendments, Lord Parkinson said Ofcom’s operational independence was key to the success of the Bill’s framework. However, the regime had to have an “an appropriate level of accountability to government”. Parliament would also have important functions, in particular scrutinising and approving the codes of practice setting out how platforms could comply with their duties and providing oversight of the Government’s powers. The Minister said he had heard the strength of feeling in Committee that the Bill’s existing provisions did not get the “balance quite right”. The Government’s amendments sought to address this by: • replacing the public policy wording with a more defined list of reasons for which the Secretary of State could make a direction – ie national security, public safety, public health and the UK’s international obligations. These were areas “genuinely beyond Ofcom’s remit as a regulator and where the Secretary of State might have access to information or expertise that the regulator does not”. • clarifying that the power would be used only for exceptional reasons. •

chunks.header: Clause 43: of HL Bill 164 621 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1745 618 619 111 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill
chunks.text: of modifications to a code. 621 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1764-5 HL Deb 12 July 2023 c1765 620 Clause 43 of HL Bill 164 621 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1745 618 619 111 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill There would also be an 18-month statutory deadline for Ofcom to submit draft codes of practice to the Secretary of State to be laid in Parliament relating to illegal content, safety duties protecting children and other cross-cutting duties. 622 The Government amended clause 39 (Secretary of State’s powers of direction) at third reading. 623 Lord Parkinson explained that the amendment would mean that, in addition to publishing a direction under the clause, the Secretary of State would also have to lay it before Parliament. These would have to be done “as soon as reasonably practicable”. 624 The Minister explained that in some cases it would be appropriate for the Secretary of State to withhold information from a laid direction – eg if publishing it would be against the interests of national security. In these cases, the Secretary of State would have to lay a statement before Parliament setting out that a direction had been given, the kind of code to which the direction related and the reasons for not publishing it. Lord Parkinson said the amendments to clauses 38 and 39 meant a “sensible and proportionate position” had been reached. 625 9.7

Lord Parkinson said this would strengthen protections for women and girls online. chunks.header: Clause 43: of HL Bill 164 621 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1745 618 619 111 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill
chunks.text: Deb 12 July 2023 c1745 Clause 44 of HL Bill 164 624 HL Deb 6 September 2023 c447 625 HL Deb 6 September 2023 c448 626 HL Deb 25 May 2023 c1040 622 623 112 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill review of existing legislation, how it related to animal welfare offences and, depending on the outcome of the review, make them priority offences under schedule 7. 627 Baroness Merron said her amendment was in response to the “worryingly high volume of animal cruelty and animal torture content” that can be found online. She explained why she had tabled the amendment: …The Government’s position appears to be that, while such activities are abhorrent, they do not generate human harm and are therefore outside the scope of this legislation. In my view, that position is undermined by some of the Government’s own amendments to this legislation, which identify content relating to animal cruelty as falling under priority harms to children. Of course, this measure is a welcome addition. However, as a number of noble Lords [have] highlighted…there is a growing body of evidence that those who engage in acts of animal cruelty go on to harm other human beings. This amendment contains a modest proposal to review whether the offences already cited from the Dispatch Box apply to online animal torture activity and, if so, to designate those offences under Schedule 7 to the Bill. We accept that the Government are already undertaking a review of criminal offences with a view to expanding the list in Schedule 7, but we have not been able to ascertain the timings attached to that review, whether its findings will be made public or whether Parliament will have a role beyond approving statutory instruments. 628

chunks.header: Clause 43: of HL Bill 164 621 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1745 618 619 111 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill
chunks.text: already undertaking a review of criminal offences with a view to expanding the list in Schedule 7, but we have not been able to ascertain the timings attached to that review, whether its findings will be made public or whether Parliament will have a role beyond approving statutory instruments. 628 For the Government, Viscount Camrose resisted the amendment. He pointed out that the Government recognised the psychological harm that animal cruelty content could cause children online. It had therefore tabled an amendment, on the first day of Report, that listed content depicting real or realistic serious violence or injury against an animal, as priority content that is harmful to children. He also noted that all services would need to proactively tackle illegal animal cruelty content where this amounted to an existing offence (eg extreme pornography). 629 Viscount Camrose said the Government had committed to further consider how the criminal law could best protect people from harmful communications, alongside other communications offences, as part of changes to the Bill made in the Commons. Harm caused by animal mistreatment communications would form part of that assessment.630 However, Viscount Camrose also said the Bill’s focus was “on ensuring that humans, in particular children, are protected online, which is why we have not listed animal offences in Schedule 7”. Listing animal offences in schedule 7 would be “likely to dilute providers’ resources away from protecting humans online, which is the Bill’s main purpose”. 631 HL Deb 12 July 2023 c1794 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1794-5 629 HL Deb 12 July 2023 c1797 630 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1797-8 631 HL Deb 12 July 2023 c1797 627 628 113 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: Deb 12 July 2023 c1794 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1794-5 629 HL Deb 12 July 2023 c1797 630 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1797-8 631 HL Deb 12 July 2023 c1797 627 628 113 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Baroness Merron’s amendment was agreed after a division (211 votes to 171). 632 The clause added by the amendment became clause 64 of the Bill as amended on report (HL Bill 164 (PDF)). 9.8 Pornography The Government amended Part 5 of the Bill (Duties of providers of regulated services: certain pornographic content) at report stage to make clear that the duties for Part 5 providers to use age verification or age estimation to prevent children accessing provider pornographic content would also extend to AIgenerated pornography. 633

chunks.header: Clause 43: of HL Bill 164 621 HL Deb 12 July 2023 cc1745 618 619 111 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill
chunks.text: Government amended Part 5 of the Bill (Duties of providers of regulated services: certain pornographic content) at report stage to make clear that the duties for Part 5 providers to use age verification or age estimation to prevent children accessing provider pornographic content would also extend to AIgenerated pornography. 633 Age assurance Government amendments relating to age assurance were also agreed at report stage. Lord Parkinson explained that these were designed to ensure that children would be prevented from accessing pornography, whether it was published by providers in scope of the Part 5 duties or allowed by userto-user services subject to Part 3 duties. The Bill would be explicit that services would “need to use highly effective age verification or age estimation to meet these new duties”. There would be a “a clear, privacy-preserving and future-proof framework” governing the use of age assurance, overseen by Ofcom. 634 The Minister said the Bill’s provisions targeting illegal content and protecting children should be implemented as soon as “feasible”. He explained that the Government’s amendments would create an 18-month statutory deadline from the day the Bill was passed for Ofcom’s implementation of those areas: …By this point, Ofcom must submit draft codes of practice to the Secretary of State to be laid in Parliament and publish its final guidance relating to illegal content duties, duties about content harmful to children and duties about pornography content in Part 5. This also includes relevant cross-cutting duties, such as content reporting procedures, which are relevant to illegal content and content harmful to children. In line with convention, most of the Bill’s substantive provisions will be commenced two months after Royal Assent. These amendments ensure that a set of specific clauses will commence earlier—on the day of Royal Assent— allowing Ofcom to begin vital implementation work sooner than it otherwise would have done. Commencing these clauses early will enable Ofcom to launch its consultation on draft codes of practice for illegal content duties shortly after Royal Assent. 635

There will also be robust definitions of age assurance, age verification and age estimation. •

chunks.text: use of age assurance on the face of the Bill, which Ofcom will be required to consider when producing codes of practice for services which allow user interaction, and guidance for sites which publish pornography. There will also be robust definitions of age assurance, age verification and age estimation. • require dedicated pornography sites (regulated under Part 5 of the Bill) to publish details of how they are protecting children from pornography. 636 Children’s risk assessment duties The Government was defeated at report stage when an amendment in the name of Baroness Kidron (Crossbench) was agreed. 637 The amendment to clause 10 (Children’s risk assessment duties) 638 would mean that in carrying out risk assessments, user-to-user services would have to “consider the potential for the design and operation of services to create harm separately and additionally to harm relating to the dissemination of or encountering harmful content”. 639 Baroness Kidron said her amendment was necessary as the Government “would not accept that harm to children can arise from the functionality and design of services and not just from the content”. The Bill’s focus on content was “old-fashioned” and looked “backwards”: … The Bill is drafted as if it has particular situations and companies in mind but does not think about how fast the business moves. When we started the Bill, none of us thought about the impact of TikTok; last week, we saw a new service, Threads, go from zero to 70 million users in a single day. chunks.header: Clause 11: of HL Bill 164 639 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1546 636 637 115 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023
chunks.text: is an act of DSIT, Factsheet on the Government’s amendments at report stage [online], 30 June 2023 Amendment 35 at HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1563-5 638 Clause 11 of HL Bill 164 639 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1546 636 637 115 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill stunning hubris to be so certain of the form of harm. To be unprepared to admit that some harm is simply design means that, despite repeated denials, this is just a content Bill. The promise of systems and processes being at the heart of the Bill has been broken. 640 Lord Parkinson disagreed. He said the Bill made it clear that “functions, features and design play a key role in the risk of harm occurring to a child online”: …I draw noble Lords’ attention to Clause 11(5), which makes it clear that the child safety duties apply across all areas of a service, including the way it is designed, operated and used, as well as content present on the service. That makes a distinction between the design, operation and use, and the content. In addition, the Bill’s online safety objectives include that regulated services should be designed and operated so as to protect from harm people in the United Kingdom who are users of the service, including with regard to algorithms used by the service, functionalities of the services and other features relating to the operation of the service. There is no reference to content in this section, again underlining that the Bill draws a distinction… 641

The promise of systems and processes being at the heart of the Bill has been broken. 640 Lord Parkinson disagreed. He said the Bill made it clear that “functions, features and design play a key role in the risk of harm occurring to a child online”: …I draw noble Lords’ attention to Clause 11(5), which makes it clear that the child safety duties apply across all areas of a service, including the way it is designed, operated and used, as well as content present on the service. That makes a distinction between the design, operation and use, and the content. In addition, the Bill’s online safety objectives include that regulated services should be designed and operated so as to protect from harm people in the United Kingdom who are users of the service, including with regard to algorithms used by the service, functionalities of the services and other features relating to the operation of the service. There is no reference to content in this section, again underlining that the Bill draws a distinction… 641

chunks.header: Clause 11: of HL Bill 164 639 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1546 636 637 115 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023
chunks.text: Worldwide, Electronic Frontier Foundation website, 26 July 2023; Security researchers latest to blast UK’s Online Safety Bill as encryption risk, Tech Crunch [online], 5 July 2023; Online Safety Bill: civil society organisations 640 641 116 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill At report stage, Lord Parkinson acknowledged concerns about how Ofcom’s powers would work with encrypted services. He said he wanted to “make clear” that the Bill would “not require companies to break or weaken encryption” and that it included “strong safeguards to ensure that users’ privacy is protected”. 647 However, given the serious risk of harm to children from sexual abuse and exploitation online, the Minister said Ofcom needed “appropriate, tightly targeted powers to compel companies to take the most effective action to tackle such reprehensible illegal activity which is taking place on their services”. He explained when Ofcom might use its powers and what would need to be considered: …The powers in the Bill are predicated on risk assessments. If companies are managing the risks on their platform appropriately, Ofcom will not need to use its powers. As a last resort, however, where there is clear evidence of child sexual abuse taking place on a platform, Ofcom will be able to direct companies either to use, or to make best efforts to develop or source, accredited and accurate technology to identify and remove this illegal content. To be clear, these powers will not enable Ofcom or our law enforcement agencies to obtain any automatic access to the content detected. It is simply a matter of making private companies take effective action to prevent child sexual abuse on their services. Ofcom must consider a wide range of matters when deciding whether a notice is necessary and proportionate, including the impacts on privacy and freedom of expression of using a particular technology on a particular service. Ofcom will only be able to require the use of technology accredited as highly accurate in detecting illegal child sexual abuse or terrorism content, vastly minimising the risk that content is wrongly identified.

chunks.header: Clause 11: of HL Bill 164 639 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1546 636 637 115 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023
chunks.text: the impacts on privacy and freedom of expression of using a particular technology on a particular service. Ofcom will only be able to require the use of technology accredited as highly accurate in detecting illegal child sexual abuse or terrorism content, vastly minimising the risk that content is wrongly identified. He continued: In addition to these safeguards, as a public body, Ofcom is bound through the Human Rights Act 1998 by the European Convention on Human Rights, including Articles 8 and 10. Ofcom has an obligation not to act in a way which unduly interferes with the right to privacy and freedom of expression when carrying out its duties, for which it is held to account. If appropriate technology does not exist which meets these requirements, Ofcom cannot require its use. That is why the powers include the ability for Ofcom to require companies to make best endeavours to develop or source a solution. It is right that we can require technology companies to use their considerable resources and expertise to develop the best possible protections for children in encrypted environments. 648 Lord Parkinson said the Government was amending the Bill to introduce additional safeguards. Ofcom would be required to obtain a skilled persons’ report before issuing a warning notice and exercising its powers. The Minister explained that this “independent expert scrutiny” would supplement Ofcom’s urge UK to protect global digital security and safeguard private communication, Open Rights Group open letter [online], 26 June 2023; WhatsApp and Signal unite against online safety bill amid privacy concerns, Guardian [online], 18 April 2023 [all accessed 31 October 2023] 647 HL Deb 19 July 2023 cc2362-3 648 HL Deb 19 July 2023 c2363 117 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.header: Clause 11: of HL Bill 164 639 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1546 636 637 115 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023
chunks.text: and Signal unite against online safety bill amid privacy concerns, Guardian [online], 18 April 2023 [all accessed 31 October 2023] 647 HL Deb 19 July 2023 cc2362-3 648 HL Deb 19 July 2023 c2363 117 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill own expertise to ensure that it had a full understanding of relevant technical issues to inform its decision-making. This would include issues specific to the service in question, such as its design and relevant factors relating to privacy.649 Ofcom would need to appoint a skilled person and notify the provider about the appointment and the relevant matters to be explored in the report before issuing its final notice. Ofcom would also have to provide a summary of the report to the relevant provider when issuing a warning notice. This would enable the provider to make representations based on Ofcom’s own analysis and that of the skilled person. 650 In response to concerns about the impact that scanning technologies could have on journalistic content and sources, Lord Parkinson said that any technology required by Ofcom would have to be highly accurate in detecting only terrorism content on public channels or only child sexual exploitation and abuse content on public or private channels. The likelihood of journalistic content or sources being compromised would therefore be low. However, to provide further reassurance, the Government was amending the Bill so that Ofcom would have to consider the impact that the use of a particular technology on a particular service would have on the availability of journalistic content and the confidentiality of journalistic sources when considering whether to issue a notice. 651 Some members of the House of Lords were not persuaded by the Government’s amendments. Lord Moylan (Conservative) claimed the Bill would put encryption at risk. 652 He also argued that “if you open a hole into end-to-end encryption…then other people can get through that hole, and the security of the system is compromised. Those other people may not be very nice; they could be hostile state actors…but they could also be our own security services and others, from whom we expect protection”. 653 Baroness Fox of Buckley (Non-Afl) said the Government should reconsider its position on encryption by the time the Bill had its third reading. She pointed to the benefits of encryption and claimed it was perverse for it to be demonised.654 At third reading, Lord Parkinson again said the Government had “no intention” to weaken the encryption technology used by platforms: …While the safety duties apply regardless of design, the Bill is clear that Ofcom cannot require companies to use proactive technology on private communications in order to comply with these duties. Ofcom can require the use of a technology by a private communication service only by issuing a notice to tackle child sexual exploitation and abuse content under Clause 122. Ofcom is also required to comply with existing data protection legislation when issuing a notice under Clause 122 and, as a public body, is bound by the Human Rights Act 1998 and the European Convention on Human Rights. When deciding whether to issue a notice, Ofcom will work closely with the service to help identify reasonable, technically feasible solutions to address child sexual exploitation and abuse risk, including drawing on evidence from a skilled persons report. If appropriate technology which meets these requirements does not exist, Ofcom cannot require its use. That is why the powers include the ability for Ofcom to require companies to make best endeavours to develop or source a new solution. It is right that Ofcom should be able to require technology companies to use their considerable resources and expertise to develop the best possible protections for children in encrypted environments. That has been our long-standing policy position. 655 9.11

That has been our long-standing policy position. 655 9.11 Transparency and accountability Clause 65 (Further duties about terms of service) would require Category 1 providers to create clear and accessible terms of service and apply them consistently and transparently. 656 The Government tightened the clause at report stage to ensure that all a provider’s terms through which they might indicate that a certain kind of content was not allowed on its service would be captured by these duties.657 Schedule 8 (Transparency reports by providers of Category 1, 2A and 2B services) was amended so that: • providers could be required by Ofcom to publish information relating to the formulation, development, and scope of user-to-user service providers’ terms of service and search service providers’ public statements of policies and procedures. This is in addition to the existing transparency provision regarding their application. • Ofcom would be able to require providers to publish more information in relation to algorithms, specifically information about the design and operation of algorithms that affect the display, promotion, restriction, discovery or recommendation of content subject to the duties in the Bill. 658 Lord Parkinson claimed the changes would enable greater public scrutiny of providers’ terms of service and their algorithms. 659 HL Deb 6 September 2023 cc457-8 Clause 73 of HL Bill 164 657 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2075 658 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2075 659 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2075 655 656 119 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.header: Clause 11: of HL Bill 164 639 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1546 636 637 115 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023
chunks.text: Deb 6 September 2023 cc457-8 Clause 73 of HL Bill 164 657 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2075 658 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2075 659 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2075 655 656 119 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill The Minister also said that, as well as holding platforms to account, Ofcom needed to be able to scrutinise algorithms’ functionalities and the other systems and processes they used. Empirical tests were a standard method for understanding the performance of an algorithmic system. They could be relevant for assessing the efficacy and wider impacts of content moderation technology, age-verification systems, and recommender systems. Government amendments to clause 91 (power to require information) 660, clause 92 (information notices) 661 and schedule 12 (Ofcom’s powers of entry, inspection and audit) were made to ensure that Ofcom would have the powers to enable it to direct and observe empirical tests remotely. This would “significantly bolster Ofcom’s ability to assess how a provider’s algorithms work, and therefore to assess its compliance with the duties in the Bill”. Lord Parkinson said the powers were “necessary and proportionate” and subject to safeguards: …First, they are limited to viewing information. Ofcom will be unable to remotely access or interfere with the service for any other purpose when exercising the power. These tests would be performed offline, meaning that they would not affect the services’ provision or the experience of users. Assessing systems, processes, features and functionalities is the focus of the powers. As such, individual user data and content are unlikely to be the focus of any remote access to view information. Additionally, the power can be used only where it is proportionate to use in the exercise of Ofcom’s functions—for example, when investigating whether a regulated service has complied with relevant safety duties. A provider would have a right to bring a legal challenge against Ofcom if it considered that a particular exercise of the power was unlawful. Furthermore, Ofcom will be under a legal obligation to ensure that the information gathered from services is protected from disclosure, unless clearly defined exemptions apply.

chunks.header: Clause 101: of HL Bill 164 Clause 103 of HL Bill 164 662 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2076 660 661 120 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill
chunks.text: A provider would have a right to bring a legal challenge against Ofcom if it considered that a particular exercise of the power was unlawful. Furthermore, Ofcom will be under a legal obligation to ensure that the information gathered from services is protected from disclosure, unless clearly defined exemptions apply. The Minister continued: The Bill contains no restriction on services making the existence and detail of the information notice public. Should a regulated service wish to challenge an information notice served to it by Ofcom, it would be able to do so through judicial review. In addition, the amendments create no restrictions on the use of this power being viewable to members of the public through a request, such as those under the Freedom of Information Act—noting that under Section 393 of the Communications Act, Ofcom will not be able to disclose information it has obtained through its exercise of these powers without the provider’s consent, unless permitted for specific, defined purposes. These powers are necessary and proportionate and will that ensure Ofcom has the tools to understand features and functionalities and the risks associated with them, and therefore the tools to assess companies’ compliance with the Bill. 662 Clause 101 of HL Bill 164 Clause 103 of HL Bill 164 662 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2076 660 661 120 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill

chunks.text: them, and therefore the tools to assess companies’ compliance with the Bill. 662 Clause 101 of HL Bill 164 Clause 103 of HL Bill 164 662 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2076 660 661 120 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill Researchers’ access to information On clause 146 (Ofcom’s report about researchers’ access to information) 663, Lord Parkinson said the Government recognised the “valuable work of researchers in improving collective understanding of the issues raised by the Bill. However, the evidence base needed developing to ensure that any sharing of sensitive information between companies and researchers could be done safely and securely. The clause was amended so that Ofcom would have to publish its report into researcher access to information within 18 months, rather than two years. The report would provide the evidence base for Ofcom guidance on the issue. According to the Minister, this would provide “valuable, evidence-based guidance on how to improve access for researchers safely and securely”. The Government would continue to explore the issue further. 664 9.12 Enforcement powers A new clause was added to the Bill at Committee stage.665 This would create an offence of non-compliance with steps set out in confirmation decisions that related to specific children’s online safety duties. Its purpose would be to ensure that providers and individuals could be held to account where their non-compliance risked serious harm to children. 666

The Government would continue to explore the issue further. 664 9.12 Enforcement powers A new clause was added to the Bill at Committee stage.665 This would create an offence of non-compliance with steps set out in confirmation decisions that related to specific children’s online safety duties. Its purpose would be to ensure that providers and individuals could be held to account where their non-compliance risked serious harm to children. 666

chunks.header: Clause 127: of the Bill as amended in Committee (HL Bill 151) 666 HL Deb 22 June 2023 c371 667 HL Deb 19 July 2023 c2389 663 664 121 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill
chunks.text: Bill at Committee stage.665 This would create an offence of non-compliance with steps set out in confirmation decisions that related to specific children’s online safety duties. Its purpose would be to ensure that providers and individuals could be held to account where their non-compliance risked serious harm to children. 666 Failure to comply with a child sexual exploitation and abuse requirement imposed by a confirmation decision At report stage, Lord Parkinson explained that concerns had been raised that the confirmation decision offence would not tackle child sexual exploitation and abuse. The Government was therefore creating a new offence of a failure to comply with a child sexual exploitation and abuse requirement imposed by a confirmation decision. This would mean that providers and senior managers could be held liable if they failed to comply with requirements to take specific steps as set out in Ofcom’s confirmation decision in relation to child sexual exploitation and abuse on their service. 667 The Minister explained: Ofcom must designate a step in a confirmation decision as a child sexual exploitation and abuse requirement, where that step relates, whether or not exclusively, to a failure to comply with specific safety duties in respect of child sexual exploitation and abuse content. Failure to comply with such a requirement will be an offence.
