This approach will ensure that services know from the confirmation decision when they risk criminal liability, while providing sufficient legal certainty via the specified steps to ensure that the offence can be prosecuted effectively. The penalty for the offence would be up to two years in prison, a fine or both. Clause 187 (liability of corporate officers) of the Bill as amended on report (HL Bill 164), would mean that where an offence was committed with the consent or connivance of a senior manager, or attributable to his or her neglect, the senior manager, as well as the entity, would have committed the offence and could face up to two years in prison, a fine or both. 668 Lord Parkinson thanked Lord Bethell as well as Miriam Cates and Sir William Cash for raising the issue in the Commons and working to strengthen the Bill in this area.669 The offence is set out in clause 139 of HL Bill 164. 9.13

chunks.text: in prison, a fine or both. 668 Lord Parkinson thanked Lord Bethell as well as Miriam Cates and Sir William Cash for raising the issue in the Commons and working to strengthen the Bill in this area.669 The offence is set out in clause 139 of HL Bill 164. 9.13 Disclosure of information about use of service by deceased child users At report stage, the Government amended the Bill to address the challenges that bereaved parents and coroners had faced when seeking to access data after the death of a child. Lord Parkinson said the amendments would create a more “straightforward and humane process for accessing data”. They would also help parents and coroners receive the answers they needed in cases where a child’s death might be related to online harms. 670 Government amendments and a new clause 671 would give Ofcom the power to require information from regulated services about a deceased child’s online activity following a request from a coroner. This would include content the child had viewed or with which they had engaged, how the content came to be encountered, the role that algorithms and other functionalities played, and the method of interaction. It would also cover any content that the child generated, uploaded, or shared on the service. The power would be backed up by Ofcom’s existing enforcement powers. Where a company refused to provide information it could face enforcement action, including senior management liability. 672 Clause 104 (disclosure of information) 673 was amended to enable Ofcom to share information with a coroner without the prior consent of a business to HL Deb 19 July 2023 c2389 HL Deb 19 July 2023 cc2389-90 670 HL Deb 10 July 2023 cc1612-3 671 Clause 102 (Information in connection with an investigation into the death of a child) of HL Bill 164 672 HL Deb 10 July 2023 cc1612-3 673 Clause 116 of HL Bill 164 668

chunks.header: Clause 102 (Information in connection with an investigation into the death of a child) of HL Bill 164 672 HL Deb 10 July 2023 cc1612-3 673 Clause 116 of HL Bill 164 668 669 122 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill disclose such information. This would ensure Ofcom was free to provide the information it collected under its existing online safety functions to coroners, as well as information requested specifically on behalf of a coroner, where that might be useful in determining whether social media played a part in a child’s death. 674 Lord Parkinson said coroners needed to have access to online safety expertise, given the technical and fast-moving nature of the industry. A new clause 675 would therefore be added to the Bill making clear that Ofcom could produce a report in connection with a person’s death, if the coroner gives Ofcom a notice or, in Scotland, the procurator fiscal requests information for that purpose. The report could include, for example, information about a company’s systems and processes, including how algorithms had promoted specific content to a child. 676 Lord Parkinson said the largest services - Category 1, 2A and 2B services would have to set out policies relating to the disclosure of data regarding the online activities of a deceased child in a clear, accessible, and sufficiently detailed format in their terms of service. They would also be required to provide a written response to data requests in a timely manner and provide a dedicated helpline (or similar means) for parents to communicate with the company. The Minister said this would address the “the painful radio silence experienced by many bereaved parents”. Companies would have to offer a complaints process for parents who believed that a platform was not meeting its obligations. These would need to be easy to access, easy to use and transparent. 677

The Bill would be amended to update Ofcom’s statutory duty to promote media literacy, in as far as it related to regulated services. 678 A new clause 679 would insert new provisions into section 11 of the Communications Act 2003 (Ofcom’s duties to promote media literacy). The Minister explained these would require Ofcom to “take steps to increase the public’s awareness and understanding of how they can keep themselves and others safe when using regulated services, including building the public’s understanding of the nature and impact of harmful content online, such as disinformation and misinformation.” Ofcom would need to carry out, commission or encourage the delivery of activities and initiatives which HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1613 Clause 164 of HL Bill 164 676 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1613 677 HL Deb 10 July 2023 c1613 678 HL Deb 17 July 2023 c2109 679 Clause 166 of HL Bill 164 674 675 123 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill enhanced users’ media literacy in these ways. When fulfilling the new objective, Ofcom would also: • need to increase the public’s awareness of the ways in which they can protect groups that disproportionately face harm online, such as women and girls. •

chunks.text: Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill enhanced users’ media literacy in these ways. When fulfilling the new objective, Ofcom would also: • need to increase the public’s awareness of the ways in which they can protect groups that disproportionately face harm online, such as women and girls. • encourage the development and use of technologies and systems that support users of regulated services to protect themselves and others. • required to publish a statement recommending ways in which others, including platforms, can take action to support their users’ media literacy. 680 A new clause 681 would require Ofcom to produce a media literacy strategy at least every three years. Ofcom’s annual report would also have to include a statement summarising and evaluating their media literacy activities, as far as they related to regulated services. 682 9.15 Power to regulate app stores The Government added a new clause 683 to give the Secretary of State the power to make regulations to amend the Bill to bring app stores within its scope. The regulations could not be made until Ofcom had published a report on the use of app stores by children (as required by clause 162 of HL Bill 164). 684 At third reading the Government amended the clause so that its reference to “age assurance” would be defined as “age verification or age estimation”. 685 9.16 Reporting and complaints procedures Government amendments were agreed at report stage. These would ensure that the Bill’s provisions on content reporting and complaints procedures would be the subject of a report from Ofcom within two years of the commencement of the Bill’s provisions. Lord Parkinson said the changes would ensure that complaints were being handled effectively.686

chunks.header: Clause 76: would be changed so that Ofcom could make provisions about qualifying
chunks.text: impose a duty on the providers of Category 1 services to arrange for and engage in an impartial, out-of-court alternative dispute resolution procedure in respect of complaints. This would mean that, if the Bill’s existing user redress provisions were found to be insufficient, the requirement could quickly be imposed.688 9.17 Regulations The Government amended clause 196 (Regulations: general) 689 at report stage so that the first regulations specifying Category 1 threshold conditions would be subject to the affirmative procedure rather than the negative. This change was recommended by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee.690 Lord Parkinson said the change to the level of parliamentary scrutiny was appropriate given that categorisation of Category 1 services would lead to the most substantial duties on the largest and most influential services. 691 Ofcom and the fees regime The costs of Ofcom’s online safety functions would be funded by annual fees. Once the fee regime was operational, regulated providers with revenue at or above a set threshold would be required to notify Ofcom and pay a proportionate fee. Ofcom would calculate fees with reference to the provider’s qualifying worldwide revenue. At report stage Lord Parkinson explained that the Government was amending the Bill’s provisions in this area in response to two recommendations made by the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee: • the Bill as drafted stated that qualifying worldwide revenue should be defined in a published statement laid before Parliament. The Committee recommended that this should be defined through regulations subject to the affirmative procedure. 692 Clause 76 would be changed so that Ofcom could make provisions about qualifying

chunks.header: Clause 76: would be changed so that Ofcom could make provisions about qualifying
chunks.text: Bill as drafted stated that the revenue threshold should be set by the Secretary of State in a published statement laid before Parliament. The committee recommended that the threshold should be set through regulations subject to the negative procedure. 694 Clause 77 would be amended to make this change. 695 Lord Parkinson also explained that Regulations to be made by Ofcom about the qualifying worldwide revenue would be to provide that the revenue accruing to certain entities in the same group as a provider of a regulated service could be considered for the purposes of determining qualifying worldwide revenue. This would enable Ofcom, when making such regulations, to make provisions, if necessary, to account for instances where a provider had a complex group structure; for example, where the regulated provider might accrue only a portion of the revenue referrable to the regulated service, the rest of which might be accrued by other entities in the group’s structure. 696 9.18

At report stage, the Government was defeated on an amendment moved by Baroness Morgan of Cotes (Labour) in relation to which services would fall within Category 1. The amendment would move from a test of size “and” functionality to a test of size “or” functionality. Baroness Morgan of Cotes said this would give Ofcom more flexibility to decide which platforms really needed Category 1 designation, because functionality could make platforms harmful: “…It is perfectly possible to have small platforms that both carry highly harmful content and themselves become harmful in the way that they are designed. 698 Lord Parkinson resisted the amendment. He claimed it could result in some large services as well as low-risk smaller services being classified as Category 1 (with the additional duties that this would bring): …[the amendment would] allow the Secretary of State by regulation to require a service to have to meet only one or other of the two requirements. That

That would create the risk that services with a high number of users but limited functionality would be caught in scope of category 1. That could be of particular concern to large websites that operate with limited functionality for public interest reasons…On the other hand, it could capture a vast array of low-risk smaller services merely because they have a specific functionality—for instance, local community fora that have livestreaming capabilities. 699 Baroness Morgan of Cotes’ amendment was agreed after a division (196 to 183 votes). 700 9.19

chunks.text: functionality for public interest reasons…On the other hand, it could capture a vast array of low-risk smaller services merely because they have a specific functionality—for instance, local community fora that have livestreaming capabilities. 699 Baroness Morgan of Cotes’ amendment was agreed after a division (196 to 183 votes). 700 9.19 Communications offences Part 10 of the Bill, which deals with a range of communications offences, has been subject to a number of Government amendments at Lords committee and report stages. These include amendments to introduce a new offence of encouraging or assisting self-harm, and four new offences relating to intimate image abuse. The Government also committed to moving an amendment at third reading to make clear that the threatening communications offence would cover indirect threats as well as direct ones. Encouraging or assisting serious self-harm The Government added a new clause to the Bill in Committee to create a new offence of encouraging or assisting serious self-harm, as had been promised by Michelle Donelan in November 2022. 701 The new clause became clause 185 (Offence of encouraging or assisting serious self-harm) of the Bill as amended on report (HL Bill 164 (PDF)). Under the new clause a person (D) would commit an offence if: • D does a “relevant act” capable of encouraging or assisting the serious self-harm of another person, and • D’s act was intended to encourage or assist the serious self-harm of another person. “Relevant act” is defined in subsection (2) and includes a range of online and in person acts of communicating, sending, showing and publishing. 699 700 701 127 HL Deb 19 July 2023 c2344 HL Deb 19 July 2023 cc2358-9 See section 4.5 of this briefing for further background. Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill “Serious self-harm” is defined as harm amounting to grievous bodily harm within the meaning of the Offences Against the Person Act 1861 (in England, Wales and Northern Ireland) or severe injury (in Scotland). Some concern was raised that the offence could inadvertently criminalise “content posted by people who are in distress and sharing their own experiences of self-harm”. 702 Some Members suggested that the offence could be amended to include defences relating to peer support and harm reduction, or a requirement for the Director of Public Prosecutions to give consent for any prosecution to occur. For the Government, Lord Parkinson said: The new offence will capture only the most serious behaviour and avoid criminalising vulnerable people who share their experiences of self-harm. The preparation of these clauses was informed by extensive consultation with interested groups and campaign bodies. 703 He went on: If a person does not intend to encourage or assist serious self-harm, as will likely be the case with recovery and supportive material, no offence will be committed. The Law Commission looked at this issue carefully, following evidence from the Samaritans and others, and the implementation will be informed by an ongoing consultation as well. 704 The new clause was agreed without a vote.

Intimate image abuse At report stage the Lords agreed a group of Government amendments relating to the non-consensual sharing of intimate images. This had been promised by the then Justice Secretary Dominic Raab in response to recommendations made by the Law Commission following a review of the law on intimate images. 705 The amendments would repeal the existing offence of disclosing (or threatening to disclose) private sexual photographs and films, as set out in section 33 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015, and replace it with four new sexual offences: • A ‘base’ offence of intentionally sharing an intimate photograph or film, or what appears to be an intimate photograph or film, without consent or reasonable belief in consent. • Two more serious offences of sharing an intimate photograph or film, or what appears to be an intimate photograph or film, without consent and Baroness Merron (Labour) at HL Deb 22 June 2023 c421 HL Deb 22 June 2023 c424 704 As above, c425 705 See section 4.6 of this briefing for further details. 702 703 128 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill either with intent to cause alarm, distress or humiliation, or for the purpose of obtaining sexual gratification. •

chunks.text: 704 As above, c425 705 See section 4.6 of this briefing for further details. 702 703 128 Commons Library Research Briefing, 31 October 2023 Online Safety Bill: Progress of the Bill either with intent to cause alarm, distress or humiliation, or for the purpose of obtaining sexual gratification. • An offence of threatening to share an intimate image or film, or what appears to be an intimate image or film, with the intention of causing fear that the threat will be carried out or being reckless as to whether such fear will be caused. The new offences have been drafted to cover manufactured or so-called ‘deepfake’ images for the first time, which Lord Parkinson said was becoming a “more prevalent” form of intimate image abuse.706 The new offences would be subject to a number of exemptions, including: • where the image was taken in public where the person in the image had no reasonable expectation of privacy and was in an intimate state voluntarily • where the image had previously been publicly shared with the consent of the person in the image • where the image is of a person under 16 and has been shared with a healthcare professional acting in that capacity • where the image is of a child in an intimate state and is “of a kind ordinarily shared between family and friends” The Government amendments were agreed without a vote. The lead amendment became clause 189 of the Bill as amended on Report (HL Bill 164 (PDF)).

As noted earlier in this Briefing, the Government was defeated on the following issues in the Lords: • adding animal welfare issues to schedule 7. 711 • amending clause 11 (Children’s risk assessment duties). This would mean that in carrying out risk assessments, user-to-user services would have to “consider the potential for the design and operation of services to create harm separately and additionally to harm relating to the dissemination of or encountering harmful content”. 712 • amending schedule 11 so that the test for determining which services would fall within Category 1 would move from a test of size “and” functionality to a test of size “or” functionality. 713 The Government agreed to the amendment to schedule 11. 714 It also agreed to add animal welfare offences to schedule 7. 715 On amending clause 11, Paul Scully, Minister for Tech and the Digital Economy, said the Government agreed with the objective, but was concerned that Baroness Kidron’s amendment might not work within the Bill’s framework. 716 The Government had therefore worked closely with Baroness Kidron and others to draft its own amendments. These would make it explicit that service providers would be required to assess the impact that design, functionalities, and other features had on the risk of harm to children. 717 The House of Lords agreed with the Commons amendments on 19 September 2023. 711 712 713 714 715 716 717 131
